what: 7. Eat — the resource intake rule (0.4.0 change)
title: tick.jl — one tick in the life of an agent
text: says. ’s grass agent’s current cell, take either max_bite worth, whichever smaller. Convert bite energy via eat_gain. Remove eaten amount cell. call eat_layered! (-op unless complex-landscape module enabled — , agents can also eat shrub canopy layers). Finally, cap agent’s energy energy_max (default 200) — agents can’t accumulate infinite reserves. Biology. foraging — central process trophic ecology. Three biological constraints encoded: Eat stand. agent gets resources current cell. ’s reaching, caching, sharing (modules add ). matches grazing herbivores well; less well predator-prey systems chase--handle norm. Handling time (max_bite). agent can process much per tick. Holling’s Type II functional response simplest form (Holling 1959). Storage capacity (energy_max). Real organisms finite storage — fat reserves, glycogen pools, gut volume. full, excess intake wasted. 0.3.x kernel single line eating: “strip cell entirely one tick.” ancestors enforced max_bite; clade outlier. Audit (Tier 1, 0.4.0) flagged restored handling time. Audit findings. 0.3.x strip--cell rule produced two artefacts: Per-tick energy windfalls. Finding rich cell yield eat_gain * grass_max = 25 energy one tick — distorting reproductive timing post-windfall agent immediately crossed min_repro_energy. Suppressed density-dependence. cell feed one agent per visit; second visitors found empty. made shared-resource scenarios (cooperation, kin altruism) harder test. max_bite = 2, maximum windfall 10 energy rich cells sustain multiple grazings. Variants worth considering. Bite size scaling body size (mentioned earlier). Different resource types (fruit, leaves, insects) different eat_gain max_bite. complex-landscape module start; adding nutritional dimensions extend . Group foraging: multiple agents cell cooperate extract (compete extract less). Currently agent eats independently turn (order side effect ag env.agents loop).
code: # 0.4.0: handling-time-limited intake. An agent extracts at most # `max_bite` units of grass from the cell per tick. Multiple ticks # (or multiple agents on consecutive ticks) are needed to deplete # a rich cell. Restores alifeR / MATLAB-Bulitko `maxbite` semantics. if env.grass[x, y] > 0.0f0     bite             = min(env.grass[x, y], max_bite)     ag.energy       += eat_gain * bite     env.grass[x, y] -= bite end eat_layered!(ag, env)   # complex landscape: shrub/canopy supplements ag.energy = min(ag.energy, Float32(get(specs, "energy_max", 200.0))) ag.energy += eat_gain * env.grass[x, y] env.grass[x, y] = 0.0f0

what: 8. Brain energy cost — thinking is expensive
title: tick.jl — one tick in the life of an agent
text: helper function: says. eating, deduct additional energy cost running brain. cost depends mode active: "none" — free thinking. cost. (Useful control runs.) "size" — cost proportional brain size. bigger brain costs maintain, regardless active . "activity" (default) — cost = size cost + activity cost. activity component scales strongly brain firing (average absolute logit value). "prediction_error" — currently placeholder, falls back size cost. Biology. Brains expensive organs. Human brains use roughly 20% total energy budget despite 2% body mass (Aiello & Wheeler 1995, Curr. Anthropol. 36:199-221). “expensive brain hypothesis” standard framework: large brains require sustained high-quality energy input maintain, constrains brain evolution. clade’s three modes encode three different theories brain cost: "size" corresponds expensive-tissue hypothesis — larger brains cost maintain (basal metabolic load). "activity" corresponds attention cost (Yaeger 1994, PolyWorld) — thinking harder costs . default adds within-lifetime selection pressure energy-efficient policies. "prediction_error" reserved information-theoretic cost (KL divergence prior posterior) isn’t implemented. MATLAB ancestor charged sum(|weights|) * annPowerCoefficient — size-based weight magnitude proxy. alifeR didn’t charge brain cost . clade’s “activity” mode biologically motivated three — novel. Audit findings. s-brain-size scenario tests parental- provisioning hypothesis: care needed bootstrap large brains newborns can’t yet forage well. Audit found effect directionally correct tiny default parameters — Δ = 0.009 units care -care. Tier 2 backlog: rebalance brain_energy_base brain_energy_activity cost consequential. Variants worth considering. Brain cost arguably scale super-linearly brain size (larger brains disproportionate maintenance costs blood supply, cooling, ). Current scaling linear. Brain cost might also depend agent — high cognitive load tasks (hunting, navigating) cost habitual ones (grazing). Currently task-dependence; activity mode closest approximation. Newborns juveniles might building costs addition running costs. Currently running cost modelled; parental-care module covers development costs separately.
code: ag.energy -= _brain_energy_cost(ag.brain, logits, e_mode, e_base, e_act) function _brain_energy_cost(brain::AbstractBrain,                               logits::Vector{Float32},                               mode::String,                               base::Float32,                               act_scale::Float32)::Float32     mode == "none" && return 0.0f0     size_cost = base * Float32(brain_size(brain))     mode == "size" && return size_cost     if mode == "activity"         act_cost = act_scale * sum(abs, logits) / Float32(length(logits))         return size_cost + act_cost     end     # prediction_error — use size cost as fallback (Phase 3 will implement KL)     size_cost end

text: ’s worth explicit tick.jl deliberately omits one tick agent’s life. responsibilities live elsewhere: Birth (reproduce.jl) — runs per tick agents ticked, scanning eligible reproducers. Death (death.jl) — runs per tick, removing agents hit death conditions (starvation, age cap, optional senescence semelparous death). Predation (modules/tick_predators.jl) — runs n_predators_init > 0. Predators update path; prey agents flagged death predators get removed kill_dead!. Optional modules — disease transmission, kin altruism, public goods, mating signals, social learning, niche construction, . lives inst/julia/src/modules/. -ops flag . separation matters: hot-path tick.jl intentionally lean, biological extensions live module files can audited reasoned isolation.

title: tick.jl — one tick in the life of an agent
text: skimming tick.jl looking biological problems, ask: energy units consistent? Movement idle costs units eating gain. Yes (abstract energy units; ratios matter, absolute values). anything happen free? — every action cost, gains require physical contact grass. ✓ hidden caps floors? Yes — energy_max (line ~118), max_bite (line ~113). biologically defensible. order operations matter? Yes — sense move means agent decides based previous surroundings, destination. biologically correct (decide go based currently see). eating happens moving, agent eats wherever ended , started. Also correct. social effects within tick? Within tick_agents! agent acts independently (awareness agents’ current-tick moves). Effects requiring interaction (predation, altruism) happen modules called ticks tick loop. standard “synchronous” ABM update; alternative (“asynchronous” — process agents one time, fully completing agent’s tick next) rarely used literature performance reasons.

title: Parameter Reference
text: six brain types differ expressive power computational cost: bnn (Bayesian neural network): default; learns distribution weights. ann: standard multilayer perceptron. ctrnn: continuous-time recurrent network; suitable temporally extended tasks. grn: gene regulatory network topology; sparse, biologically motivated. transformer: self-attention architecture; highest capacity, slowest. synthesis: symbolic rule extraction evolved weights.

title: Parameter search — agent-level parameters
text: 15 TRAIT_* scalar traits live genome express per individual. silent (fixed biologically neutral default) unless _evolution flag TRUE. can search either init mean (ask population starts) _mutation_sd (ask fast drifts), . _evolution flag FALSE, init-mean mutation-SD ignored. trait clamped biologically neutral value (usually 1.0 multipliers, 0.0 zero-centred propensities). catches beginners: setting body_size_init_mean = 2.0 body_size_evolution = FALSE effect. fidelity audit pace--life trace cautionary example exact pitfall.

title: Parameter search — agent-level parameters
text: Four architecture knobs, searchable: brain_type categorical — search looping conditions rather passing CMA-ES. bnn_sigma_source also categorical; two non-default modes introduced 0.4.0 specifically make Baldwin Effect testable (see kernel-0.4.0.md).

title: Parameter search — agent-level parameters
text: max_age_scales_with_metabolism = TRUE lets pace--life scenarios show predicted fast-pace-short-life pattern; otherwise every agent dies base cap regardless metabolic rate.

title: Parameter search — agent-level parameters
text: 0.4.0 rewrote reproduction-cost accounting. legacy fixed mode still available reproducing pre-0.4.0 results, biological default now proportional (Smith & Fretwell 1974). female_investment coupling new 0.4.0 — -op . See s-parental-investment ρ = −1.00 dose-response now produces.

title: Parameter search — agent-level parameters
text: Kin altruism textbook case clean parameter search: 9-cell B × C grid fixed r_min = 0.25 recovers Hamilton’s rule Spearman ρ = 0.97, similar grid cooperation multiplier recovers Nowak–May ρ = 1.00. See s-kin s-cooperation.

what: Worked example: random search over agent parameters
title: Parameter search — agent-level parameters
text: cheapest way explore — 30 simulations random points inside hyper-box specify. Use first locate productive regions committing expensive algorithm. result data frame sorted descending score. row one simulation; score mean genetic_diversity across ticks. Retrieve full specs best configuration:
code: library(clade)  result <- search_random(   specs_base    = default_specs(),   search_params = list(     mutation_sd             = c(0.01, 0.5),     crossover_rate          = c(0.0, 2.0),     body_size_mutation_sd   = c(0.01, 0.2)   ),   n_samples = 30L,   objective = "genetic_diversity" )  head(result) best_specs <- attr(result, "specs_list")[[1]] cat("Best mutation_sd:", best_specs$mutation_sd, "\n") cat("Best crossover_rate:", best_specs$crossover_rate, "\n")

what: Worked example: CMA-ES on brain hyperparameters
title: Parameter search — agent-level parameters
text: want single best brain configuration search space continuous (BNN prior width, learning rate, body-size trait), CMA-ES gives good answer ~20 iterations × popsize 10 = 200 runs. Use learning_rate_evolution = TRUE + rl_mode = "actor_critic" combination test Baldwin Effect directly — exactly setup auto-calibration harness dev/audit/calibration/ uses.
code: result <- search_cmaes(   specs_base = default_specs(),   params     = c("bnn_sigma_init",                  "learning_rate_init_mean",                  "body_size_mutation_sd"),   objective  = "genetic_diversity",   n_iterations = 20L,   popsize      = 10L )  cat("Best diversity:", result$score, "\n") print(result$specs[c("bnn_sigma_init",                      "learning_rate_init_mean",                      "body_size_mutation_sd")])

title: Parameter search — agent-level parameters
text: domain-specific tune_iffolk() wrapper pre-configures relevant agent-level parameters (transfer rate, parliament cost, helper tendency evolution) uses objective_iffolk(), fits linear regression mean_helper_tendency time rewards upward trends plus per-agent transfer bonus. far sensitive simply maximising final helper tendency: Pair search_viability() first — see ps-environment-parameters.html#viability-mapping — extreme parameter values IFfolk population goes extinct CMA-ES signal follow.
code: tuned_iff <- tune_iffolk(default_specs(), n_iterations = 80L) tuned_iff$specs$iffolk_transfer   # optimal transfer amount

Niche-construction parameters (shelter_build_prob, shelter_occupancy_bonus) agent-behavioural, effect grass regrowth environmental. Scavenging (carrion_eat_gain) agent foraging world state created another agent’s death.

title: Parameter search — agent-level parameters
text: ps-introduction.html — search, algorithm, design objective function. ps-environment-parameters.html — grid, resources, seasonality, external forces. ps-algorithms.html — full algorithm reference. vignette("parameter-reference") — exhaustive list every parameter default_specs().

title: Parameter search — algorithms
text: . cheap initial screen committing expensive algorithm. priors good region needed. . Samples n_samples points uniformly hyper-rectangle specify, runs simulation , scores , returns sorted descending score. Cost. 30 simulations × 5–30 s = 2–15 minutes default settings. Retrieve full specs best row: Strengths. Embarrassingly parallel, needs priors, easy reason , gives partial-dependence plots free -product. Weaknesses. Scales badly dimension (coverage drops fast beyond 5 parameters). Doesn’t exploit structure; two parameters interact, random search won’t exploit .
code: library(clade)  result <- search_random(   specs_base    = default_specs(),   search_params = list(     mutation_sd   = c(0.01, 0.5),     grass_rate    = c(0.05, 0.8),     n_agents_init = c(20L, 150L)   ),   n_samples  = 30L,   objective  = "genetic_diversity" )  head(result) #>   mutation_sd grass_rate n_agents_init score #> 1       0.312      0.621           127 0.847 #> 2       0.287      0.583           119 0.831 #> 3       0.341      0.548           143 0.819 best_specs <- attr(result, "specs_list")[[1]] cat("Best mutation_sd:", best_specs$mutation_sd, "\n") cat("Best grass_rate:", best_specs$grass_rate, "\n")

title: Parameter search — algorithms
text: . want understand achievable set outcomes, just single best point. MAP-Elites fills grid niches behavioural space reports best parameter set niche. . Divides behavioural space (two dimensions get_run_data()$ticks columns) bins. Starts random parameter sets, iteration picks existing archive member, perturbs , runs simulation, places result whichever bin falls — iff bin empty new score beats incumbent. Cost. 300 simulations = 20–90 minutes. Interpreting heatmap. Filled cells show achievable (diversity, population) pairs. colour encodes objective score. Empty cells (grey) combinations simulated parameter set produced. high-diversity, large-population cells empty, combination may intrinsically unattainable. raw archive (result$archive) list length equal number cells. Non-null entries $specs, $score, $behavioural_descriptor. Inspect archive directly: Strengths. Illumination — reveals Pareto-like frontiers impossible regions single-objective methods silently skip . Robust local optima. Weaknesses. Expensive per cell filled. Needs meaningful bin definitions; archive dims don’t carve space usefully, result uninformative. Known issue (0.4.0 backlog). default mutation, MAP-Elites sometimes fills one cell — candidate mutations produce near- identical behavioural descriptors. See s-map-elites audit workaround (wider mutation + broader archive bins).
code: result <- search_map_elites(   specs_base   = default_specs(),   archive_dims = list(     genetic_diversity = seq(0, 1, by = 0.1),     n_agents          = seq(0, 200, by = 20)   ),   n_iterations = 300L,   objective    = "genetic_diversity",   verbose      = FALSE )  # Archive heatmap: x = genetic_diversity niche, y = n_agents niche, # fill = objective score (NA = empty cell). result$map non_empty <- Filter(Negate(is.null), result$archive) cat("Filled cells:", length(non_empty), "\n") cat("mutation_sd range in archive:",     range(sapply(non_empty, function(x) x$specs$mutation_sd)), "\n")

title: Parameter search — algorithms
text: . want single best parameter combination one scalar objective, parameters continuous (discrete categorical choices like brain_type). . Maintains multivariate Gaussian parameter space. generation samples popsize candidates, runs , uses ranked top half update Gaussian’s mean covariance. step size (sigma) shrinks search converges. clade’s search_cmaes() pure-R implementation (Hansen 2006) — external packages required. Cost. n_iterations × popsize simulations = 200–500, .e. 15–90 minutes. Strengths. State art continuous derivative-free optimisation. Converges faster random search, handles ill-conditioned objectives. Treats run_alife() black box — discrete events (death, birth) obstacle. Weaknesses. Returns single point, trade-map. Can get stuck local optima (mitigated running multiple restarts different seeds). Doesn’t handle categorical parameters (brain_type, life_history). Rule thumb choosing CMA-ES MAP-Elites: use CMA-ES care optimum ; use MAP-Elites care landscape looks like around optimum. CMA-ES faster per run single-objective answer; MAP-Elites informative per compute budget multi-objective insight.
code: result <- search_cmaes(   specs_base = default_specs(),   params     = c("mutation_sd", "grass_rate", "crossover_rate"),   objective  = "genetic_diversity",   n_iterations = 20L,   popsize    = 10L )  cat("Best diversity:", result$score, "\n") cat("Best params:\n") print(result$specs[c("mutation_sd", "grass_rate", "crossover_rate")])

previous_headings: CMA-ES
title: Parameter search — algorithms
text: per-scenario CMA-ES harness ships dev/audit/calibration/. s-*.Rmd scenario fitness function encoding biological claim (e.g. “negative slope mean_prior_sigma” Baldwin Effect, “late n_altruistic_acts per tick” kin selection). Running bash dev/audit/calibration/run_all.sh launches 31 parallel (xargs -P 16) writes per-scenario JSON best-discovered specs. Results summarised dev/audit/calibration/RESULTS.md. Several scenarios retracted prior “Calibrated regime” claims 0.4.0 fidelity audit found CMA-ES optimised generic fitness rather stated theoretical signal — see ps-introduction.html discipline requires.

title: Parameter search — algorithms
text: . CMA-ES region ’re sure population survives . Also useful standalone sanity-check setting new scenario. . Runs rectangular 2-parameter grid n_reps replicates per cell. Reports ggplot heatmap coloured viability (green = population survives; red = extinction) tidy data frame. Cost. length(values_x) × length(values_y) × n_reps simulations. 5 × 5 × 3 = 75 runs, 5–15 minutes. Strengths. Clearest possible picture system alive. Runs fast extinction-prone cells finish early. Cheap enough run every time set new scenario. Weaknesses. 2 parameters time (design — heatmap output). Doesn’t optimise; just shows ’s safe optimise.
code: library(clade)  s <- default_specs() s$complex_landscape <- TRUE  vmap <- search_viability(   s,   param_x = "shrub_density",  values_x = seq(0.1, 0.6, by = 0.1),   param_y = "canopy_density", values_y = seq(0.05, 0.35, by = 0.1),   n_reps  = 3L )  vmap$map     # ggplot2 heatmap vmap$data    # data frame with viability, mean_final_pop per cell

previous_headings: Viability search
title: Parameter search — algorithms
text: two scenarios viability mapping particularly worthwhile, clade ships domain-specific tuners combine viability mapping CMA-ES pass inside viable region: tune_complex_landscape() — five landscape parameters + custom objective measuring joint increase wing size, niche diversity, survival. tune_iffolk() — IFfolk transfer rate parliament cost + custom objective rewarding upward trends mean_helper_tendency.

title: Parameter search — algorithms
text: . Final-tick refinement objective surface smooth near known good point — want fractional improvement parameter already trust. . Estimates gradient objective respect parameter finite differences, takes step positive direction. Repeats step size drops threshold. Cost. n_iterations × (n_params × 2 + 1) simulations per iteration (two finite-difference evaluations per parameter plus one midpoint). Rarely efficient option. Strengths. Simple. Good last 1–5% optimisation CMA-ES found basin. Weaknesses. Gets stuck local optima. Sensitive step size. Doesn’t handle noisy objectives well (clade’s simulations stochastic, objectives noisy). Usage mirrors CMA-ES signature:
code: result <- search_gradient(   specs_base   = default_specs(),   params       = c("mutation_sd", "grass_rate"),   objective    = "genetic_diversity",   n_iterations = 20L,   step_size    = 0.02 )

title: Parameter search — algorithms
text: Julia mature automatic differentiation (Zygote.jl, Enzyme.jl). natural ask whether differentiate run_alife() end--end use gradient descent. . run_alife() contains several operations differentiable: Agent death threshold event: energy < starvation_threshold. Reproduction conditional energy >= min_repro_energy. Action selection uses argmax brain logits. Mating categorical sample. Autodiff either fails trace time silently produces zero gradient. correct class optimisers stochastic discrete-event simulations derivative-free: CMA-ES, MAP-Elites, random sampling. treat simulator black box estimate curvature population statistics.

title: Parameter search — algorithms
text: five search functions accept n_cores argument (default 1). Set number R workers want clade fan inner simulations across. n_iterations × popsize = 200 simulations, 4-core machine can cut wall time ~40 minutes ~10 minutes. Julia caches precompiled simulation package per worker, first call worker pays ~60-second compile cost. batches 10 simulations per worker, overhead amortised; smaller batches, consider running fewer cores using batch_alife() warm Julia sessions first.

what: Choosing the right algorithm — a decision tree
title: Parameter search — algorithms
code: Is the population surviving at all at the starting point? ├── No  → search_viability() first. └── Yes → Do you want one best answer or a landscape?     ├── One best answer     │   ├── Parameters continuous?    → search_cmaes()     │   └── Parameters categorical?   → loop over conditions     │                                   with batch_alife()     └── A landscape         ├── 2D trade-offs known in advance  → search_map_elites()         └── Exploring without priors        → search_random()                                               (then iterate)

title: Parameter search — environment-level parameters
text: usually aren’t parameters search — ’re ones set experiment. exception max_ticks: simulation cost scales linearly ticks, coarse--fine search workflow uses short max_ticks values (200–500) search long max_ticks (1000+) winning configuration’s final verification.

title: Parameter search — environment-level parameters
text: grass_rate single consequential environmental parameter. Low values (0.02–0.05) give scarce environment exposes life-history trade-offs; high values (0.2–0.5) give abundant environment density-dependent effects dominate. scenario audits dev/audit/fidelity/ sweep explicitly. max_bite new 0.4.0. implements handling time — rich cell stripped one step. matters ecological-release scenarios: max_bite = grass_max, forager finds rich cell wins everything; smaller max_bite, cell sustains multiple grazings across ticks. See kernel-0.4.0.md biology.

title: Parameter search — environment-level parameters
text: Complex-landscape parameters classic “map region optimise” case. Many combinations drive population extinct (little productive area, wrong energy ratios), CMA-ES follow gradient dead region. Use search_viability() first — see worked example . seasonal_amplitude interacts multiplicatively grass_rate: effective_rate_t = grass_rate × (1 + seasonal_amplitude × sin(2π t / season_length)). signature s-seasonal lag-season_length/2 anti-correlation grass_coverage.

title: Parameter search — environment-level parameters
text: Predators agents brains lineages, handful parameters control abundance lethality best read environmental selection forces prey population. per-predator traits (predator_attack_strength, predator_energy_gain) closer agent parameters — see ps-agent-parameters.html#cross-links--fuzzy-parameters.

title: Parameter search — environment-level parameters
text: Disease state (infected/recovered) lives agents, transmission mechanics live environment. transmission_prob R0-equivalent knob. s-disease scenario audit showed Spearman ρ = 1.00 dose-response transmission_prob epidemic peak — one cleanest theoretical signals suite.

title: Parameter search — environment-level parameters
text: running CMA-ES parameter region, worth mapping viable region — set parameter combinations population doesn’t go extinct. search_viability() single call returns heatmap tidy data frame. viable region identified, run CMA-ES inside : tune_complex_landscape() pre-configured wrapper: sets five landscape parameters (shrub_density, canopy_density, shrub_energy, canopy_energy, shrub_growth_rate) uses objective_complex_landscape(), measures joint increase wing size, niche diversity, survival.
code: library(clade)  s <- default_specs() s$complex_landscape <- TRUE  vmap <- search_viability(   s,   param_x = "shrub_density",  values_x = seq(0.1, 0.6, by = 0.1),   param_y = "canopy_density", values_y = seq(0.05, 0.35, by = 0.1),   n_reps  = 3L )  vmap$map     # ggplot2 heatmap: green = viable, red = extinct vmap$data    # data frame with viability, mean_final_pop per cell tuned <- tune_complex_landscape(default_specs(), n_iterations = 80L) tuned$specs          # optimal parameter set tuned$history$sigma  # step-size decay — smaller sigma = converged

Reading heatmap: empty cells combinations seasonal regime achieved. high-diversity, high-population cells empty, combination may intrinsically unattainable — seasonality may preclude simultaneous high diversity high density.
code: result <- search_map_elites(   specs_base   = default_specs(),   search_params = list(     seasonal_amplitude = c(0.0, 1.0),     season_length      = c(20L, 200L)   ),   archive_dims = list(     genetic_diversity = seq(0, 1, by = 0.1),     n_agents          = seq(0, 200, by = 20)   ),   n_iterations = 300L,   objective    = "genetic_diversity",   verbose      = FALSE )  result$map   # heatmap of achievable niches

Niche-construction effects grass regrowth — shelter-building agent behaviour, resulting grass-suppression environmental consequence searchable via grass_rate × niche_* interactions. Carrion (carrion_fraction, carrion_decay_rate) — state environment created agent deaths; see ps-agent-parameters.html#cross-links--fuzzy-parameters.

title: Parameter search — environment-level parameters
text: ps-introduction.html — search, algorithm, design objective function. ps-agent-parameters.html — traits, brain, life history, reproduction, social behaviour. ps-algorithms.html — full algorithm reference. vignette("parameter-reference") — exhaustive list every parameter default_specs().

what: Why search the parameter space at all?
title: Parameter search — introduction
text: clade exposes roughly 90 parameters (see vignette("parameter-reference")). Even biologically defensible default, serious experiment asks question form parameter combination produces outcome X? — “outcome X” rarely single parameter turned . Examples motivate searching: Discovery experiments. “grass-renewal rate × metabolic-rate combination produces largest sustained population?” Scenario calibration. “toxin_dose × avoid_threshold Müllerian aposematism actually evolve, rather drift?” Regime mapping. “viable region complex-landscape module — shrub_density × canopy_density combinations avoid population collapse?” Trade-visualisation. “achievable set (genetic diversity, population size) pairs? high diversity low population size option, can large simultaneously?” questions maps different search algorithm. single “tune ” button wrong tool .

what: The four tools at a glance
title: Parameter search — introduction
text: clade parameter search functions glance. typical workflow moves coarse fine: search_viability() — sure default region even sustains life, map first. search_random() — cheap 30-run sweep find roughly productive regions parameter space. search_map_elites() — illuminate trade-offs among top candidates step 2. search_cmaes() — zoom cell care find single best point. search_gradient() — optional final-tick refinement objective surface smooth near optimum. users live steps 2 3. others specialist tools.

what: What gets searched: agent vs environment parameters
title: Parameter search — introduction
text: parameter default_specs() usually one two kinds, two kinds imply different experimental designs different choices search method. dedicate vignette : Agent-level parameters — anything varies per organism per species: heritable traits (body_size_*, metabolic_rate_*, brain_size_*), brain architecture (brain_type, bnn_sigma_*, rl_mode), life-history (max_age, senescence_rate, life_history), reproduction (repro_cost_*, offspring_energy_*, clutch_size_*, female_investment), mutation (mutation_sd, stress_mutation_multiplier), signals mate choice (signal_dims, mate_choice_*), social behaviours (kin_altruism_*, iffolk_*, parliament_*, cooperation_*). predator params, disease + disease params). two categories overlap edges: predators, disease, niche construction sit agents world meet. cases flag fuzzy parameter pages cross-link rather pick side.

title: Parameter search — introduction
text: Every search function accepts either column name (get_run_data()$ticks) custom function takes run environment returns scalar. custom function form important one: separates valid scientific search fitness-hacking exercise. Good objective functions clade typically three properties. 1. measure biology actually claim. scenario’s biological claim “Hamilton’s rule predicts population rises rB/C”, objective Spearman correlation rB/C mean_n_agents across grid B C values — population size single parameter point. guards Goodhart’s law, earlier CMA-ES-calibrated regimes scenario vignettes fell victim . 2. penalise degenerate outcomes. run goes extinct tick 50 rewarded transient high diversity. canonical pattern : penalises extinction, smooths score surface, keeps optimiser “high-diversity-just--collapse” trap. 3. insensitive run length . want “equilibrium diversity”, average second half run rather whole run — first half dominated initial-condition transients. custom_obj can passed unchanged search_random(), search_map_elites(), search_cmaes(), search_gradient().
code: custom_obj <- function(env) {   d   <- get_run_data(env)$ticks   div <- mean(d$genetic_diversity, na.rm = TRUE)   pop <- mean(d$n_agents,          na.rm = TRUE)   if (pop < 10) return(0)      # discard runs that go near-extinct   div * log1p(pop / 50)        # reward diversity weighted by log-population }

title: Parameter search — introduction
text: Julia excellent automatic differentiation (Zygote.jl, Enzyme.jl). tempting ask whether clade’s simulator differentiated end--end optimised gradient descent. , good biological reasons. run_alife() contains several operations differentiable: Agent death threshold event: energy < starvation_threshold triggers removal. gradient Bernoulli draw w.r.t. energy zero almost everywhere. Reproduction conditional energy >= min_repro_energy — another threshold. Action selection uses argmax brain’s output logits. argmax zero gradient w.r.t. logits almost everywhere. Mating categorical sample, zero-gradient operation. Autodiff either fails trace time silently produces zero gradient. correct optimisers derivative-free — CMA-ES, MAP-Elites, random sampling — treat run_alife() black box estimate curvature population statistics alone.

title: Parameter search — introduction
text: practical constraint: run_alife() call takes roughly 5–30 seconds modern machine (default grid, 500 ticks). typical search therefore involves ~300 runs costs tens minutes hour. Two ways multiply throughput: Use n_cores > 1 search functions. clade fan inner simulations across R workers. Use smaller max_ticks search (300–500) re-run winning candidate full length (1,000+) confirm signal transient. typically doubles effective throughput. Avoid running CMA-ES MAP-Elites max_ticks > 1000 know winning region; coarse--fine workflow cheaper.

title: Evolution of bad science
text: models. pure-R simulation (Julia required) Smaldino & McElreath (2016). n_labs labs two heritable traits: research power WW (probability detecting true effect hypothesis test real) research effort ee (methodological rigour). tick, lab tests n_studies_per_tick hypotheses, priori true probability base_rate_true (bb). per-test false-positive rate α(e)=αbase(1−e)\alpha(e) = \alpha_{\text{base}} (1 - e) — depends effort , WW, matching Smaldino & McElreath’s formulation. publication pressure top-50%--publications labs reproduce tick; low-effort labs accumulate publications via false positives, low ee spreads selection. replication_rate > 0, lab probability per tick replicating random peer’s random finding. failed replication (original false positive, probability α(1−b)/(Wb+α(1−b))\alpha(1-b) / (Wb + \alpha(1-b))) debits original lab’s publication count replication_penalty (default 5), operationalising reputational cost published--overturned findings. S&M 2016 predicted. () Without replication, effort declines FPR rises ~100–400 generations. (ii) Replication slows decay rate increases, fully reverse realistic rates. (iii) Structural reform incentives (just replication) needed fix problem. Evolution bad science replication penalty (mean 10 seeds). Top: false-positive rate. Bottom: research effort. -replication (red): FPR rises 0.10 0.147, effort falls 0.71 — canonical S&M result. 50% replication (blue): FPR stabilises near 0.10, effort holds 0.81 — replication culture selects low-effort labs. 10% replication (orange): FPR overshoots 0.18 — weak replication worse none, finding model reported S&M. Julia session required. found. Running run_bad_science(n_ticks = 500L, replication_rate = rr) across 10 seeds (seed = 1..10) three replication rates, conditions start FPR = 0.100, effort = 0.801: Three take-aways: Bad science evolves unchecked (rr = 0): effort erodes, FPR rises — reproducing S&M’s main finding. Strong replication works (rr = 0.5): FPR stays baseline levels, effort holds near initial value. Replication culture can discipline research norms penalty real sampling frequent. Weak replication worse none (rr = 0.1): FPR overshoots -replication. finding model S&M report — possibly discrete-time sampling introduces noise , low replication rates, pushes selection threshold needed discriminate effort. listed discovery experiment 1 .
code: # No Julia required — runs entirely in R library(ggplot2) library(patchwork)  rep_rates  <- c(0.0, 0.1, 0.5) rep_labels <- c("No replication", "10% replication", "50% replication")  results <- mapply(function(rr, lab) {   df      <- run_bad_science(n_ticks = 500L, replication_rate = rr, seed = 42L)   df$rate <- lab   df }, rep_rates, rep_labels, SIMPLIFY = FALSE)  df <- do.call(rbind, results)  p1 <- ggplot(df, aes(t, mean_fpr, colour = rate)) +   geom_line() +   labs(title = "False-positive rate under publication pressure",        x = "Tick", y = "Mean FPR", colour = NULL) +   theme_minimal()  p2 <- ggplot(df, aes(t, mean_effort, colour = rate)) +   geom_line() +   labs(title = "Research effort",        x = "Tick", y = "Mean effort", colour = NULL) +   theme_minimal()  p1 / p2 + plot_layout(guides = "collect") &   theme(legend.position = "bottom")

title: Evolution of bad science
text: baseline reproduces S&M 2016 qualitatively: bad science evolves unchecked, strong replication disciplines . Open questions explore: weak-replication penalty. 10% replication-rate condition FPR higher -replication control — weak replication worse none. artefact discrete-time sampling (tick Bernoulli draw whether replication happens), persist continuous-time formulations? Sweep replication_rate 0.02 0.5 0.02 steps replication_penalty = 5 look crossover. Penalty magnitude. replication penalty single scalar. Sweep 0 20 fixed replication_rate = 0.5 locate dose- response curve. threshold replication effect, continuous? Smaldino’s original paper treats penalty tied reputation cost retraction — magnitude corresponds typical academic retractions? Structural reform: base_rate_true. Replication fails often published findings true positives. Raising base_rate_true 0.3 0.7 (simulating field pre-registers hypotheses tests well-motivated ones) make replication far effective failed-replication probability now discriminates low- high-effort labs sharply. Verify sweeping bb fixed replication_rate = 0.1 — weak replication start help hypothesis prevalence high enough? Structural heterogeneity. Run mixture lab types — minority high-effort labs never cut corners (effort fixed 0.95). presence honest minority slow reverse FPR drift? Vary minority fraction 5% 30% using batch_alife()-style replicate loops. Tried . 10% fixed-high-effort minority (effort = 0.95 20 200 labs, 500 ticks, seed 42): FPR ended 0.267 vs 0.283 (minority) — 5.7% reduction. minority slightly retards FPR evolution maintaining higher-effort tail competes successfully random replication occasionally evaluates work. 10% insufficient reverse drift: 90% low-effort majority still dominates. 30% fixed high-effort: FPR = 0.241 (-15%). 30% structural minority meaningful intervention — roughly “critical mass” fraction needed stabilise scientific norms model.

what: BNN uncertainty canalization — the Baldwin Effect
title: BNN uncertainty canalization and the Baldwin Effect
text: models. Bayesian neural network (BNN) brain type (brain_type = "bnn") places prior distribution synaptic weight, parameterised mean μ standard deviation σ. large σ encodes behavioural flexibility: agent samples widely weight posterior can learn within lifetime via Thompson sampling. small σ encodes genetic assimilation: agent’s behaviour largely determined prior mean — population , generations selection, canalized formerly learned behaviour genome. computational signature Baldwin Effect (Baldwin 1896), formalised Hinton & Nowlan (1987): learning guides evolution toward fixed adaptive solutions, learning machinery becomes redundant. Mayley (1996) showed analytically speed canalization depends stability fitness landscape mutational variance available selection act . BNN brain clade implements mechanistically: σ derived heterozygosity (half absolute difference maternal paternal alleles weight locus), high genome diversity implies broad priors high within-lifetime flexibility, genetic convergence produces narrow priors increasingly instinctive behaviour. σ changes time. Two mechanisms act σ within agent’s lifetime: Posterior contraction (rl_mode = "actor_critic"): tick, posterior σ active weights shrinks proportional learning signal. within-lifetime narrowing resets birth — offspring inherit prior σ genome, posterior. Genetic assimilation (across generations): offspring σ reflects parental heterozygosity. high-σ (exploratory) agents leave fewer offspring low-σ (exploitative) agents, mean σ declines generations even without within-lifetime learning. Baldwin Effect proper. key observable. Track mean_prior_sigma data$ticks. Declining σ ticks = canalization / Baldwin Effect. Rising stable σ = selection maintains flexibility. Key parameters.

what: Baseline: does the Baldwin Effect occur in a foraging world?
title: BNN uncertainty canalization and the Baldwin Effect
text: Expected output (theoretical). evolutionary time, prior σ decline selection canalizes learned behaviour. Audit caveat (2026-04-15). 4-seed × 800-tick audit found σ rises ~0.19 cap 0.50 stable seasonal environments — opposite Baldwin prediction. Root cause: clade’s σ derived per-locus heterozygosity (|maternal − paternal|), accumulates neutral mutation regardless selection pressure. Without kernel change decouple σ heterozygosity add explicit cost uncertainty, Baldwin canalization observed default parameters. See dev/audit/fidelity/baldwin.md.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  s <- default_specs() s$brain_type <- "bnn" s$max_ticks  <- 600L s$random_seed <- 42L  env  <- run_alife(s) data <- get_run_data(env)  ggplot(data$ticks, aes(x = t, y = mean_prior_sigma)) +   geom_line(colour = "#2166ac", linewidth = 0.8, alpha = 0.7) +   geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE,               colour = "#d6604d", fill = "#f4a582", alpha = 0.4) +   labs(     title    = "Genetic assimilation of learned behaviour (Baldwin Effect)",     subtitle = "BNN prior sigma over time — declining = canalization, rising = flexibility favoured",     x = "Tick", y = expression("Mean prior " * sigma)   ) +   theme_minimal()

title: BNN uncertainty canalization and the Baldwin Effect

See dev/audit/calibration/RESULTS.md full CMA-ES results. BNN prior sigma trajectory. Declining sigma indicates canalization (Baldwin Effect); rising sigma indicates selection maintained behavioural flexibility. found. Running brain_type = "bnn", 80 agents, 25×25 grid, grass_rate = 0.15, 600 ticks (seed 42): mean_prior_sigma rose monotonically 0.30 ticks 1–50 0.50 (maximum ceiling) tick 300, remained rest run. Sigma moved upward, downward — opposite Baldwin Effect canalization. result failure implementation. theoretically coherent finding: spatially heterogeneous environment single optimal foraging policy, maintaining exploration adaptively superior fixing particular strategy. fitness landscape stable enough canalization proceed. Canalization requires fitness landscape stable global optimum — condition dynamic foraging world population competition provide short run lengths. Theoretical interpretation. Mayley (1996) showed Baldwin Effect operates fastest (1) fitness landscape single sharp peak, (2) mutational variance sufficient selection find , (3) generation times long enough selection act. competitive foraging world spatial heterogeneity stochastic resource patches, condition (1) fails: optimal foraging strategy shifts continuously population density grass distribution. Selection therefore maintains ability learn (high σ) evolutionarily stable strategy.

title: BNN uncertainty canalization and the Baldwin Effect
text: Question. environmental predictability determine whether canalization occurs? theory predicts : stable, resource-rich environments provide consistent fitness gradients canalization can track; variable scarce environments . found. 3 × 3 factorial design (grass_rate × seasonal_amplitude), 5 seeds , 1000 ticks per run (45 runs total). Every single condition produced positive sigma slope — canalization cell. 9 conditions converged ceiling value (σ = 0.5) tick 1000. Crucially, slope ordering inverse Baldwin Effect prediction: resource scarcity (grass_rate = 0.05) produced steepest positive slopes (fastest σ rise), resource abundance (grass_rate = 0.20) produced shallowest — still positive — slopes. foraging difficult, agents broad priors (high σ) can explore widely strong survival advantage; tight priors fatal. Seasonal amplitude minimal additional effect within resource level. one exception: grass_rate = 0.05, seasonal_amplitude = 0.4 showed mean slope 2.8× higher neighbours sd_slope 378× higher — reflecting stochastic population crashes resource-variability combination, canalization. ‡ High variance across seeds (sd_slope 378× larger conditions) — population-crash artefact, canalization. Bold row = smallest positive slope = canalization-favourable condition tested. Even , σ rose ceiling. Experiment 2 tests whether longer runs (2000–5000 ticks) change outcome. Phase diagram sigma slope across 9 resource × seasonality conditions. cells positive (red) — exploration selected every environment tested. shallowest slope (bottom-right, bold row) still reaches sigma ceiling.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2) library(patchwork)  make_specs <- function(grass_rate, seasonal_amplitude) {   s <- default_specs()   s$brain_type         <- "bnn"   s$n_agents_init      <- 100L   s$grid_rows          <- 25L   s$grid_cols          <- 25L   s$max_ticks          <- 1000L   s$grass_rate         <- grass_rate   s$seasonal_amplitude <- seasonal_amplitude   s }  conditions <- expand.grid(   grass_rate         = c(0.05, 0.10, 0.20),   seasonal_amplitude = c(0.0,  0.4,  0.8) )  # Run each condition across 5 seeds results <- vector("list", nrow(conditions)) for (i in seq_len(nrow(conditions))) {   results[[i]] <- batch_seeds(     make_specs(conditions$grass_rate[i], conditions$seasonal_amplitude[i]),     seeds = 1:5   ) }  # Extract slopes slopes <- mapply(function(res, i) {   seeds_data <- lapply(res, function(env) get_run_data(env)$ticks)   slopes_per_seed <- sapply(seeds_data, function(d)     coef(lm(mean_prior_sigma ~ t, data = d))["t"])   data.frame(     grass_rate         = conditions$grass_rate[i],     seasonal_amplitude = conditions$seasonal_amplitude[i],     mean_slope         = mean(slopes_per_seed),     se_slope           = sd(slopes_per_seed) / sqrt(5)   ) }, results, seq_len(nrow(conditions)), SIMPLIFY = FALSE) slopes_df <- do.call(rbind, slopes)  ggplot(slopes_df, aes(x = factor(grass_rate), y = factor(seasonal_amplitude),                       fill = mean_slope)) +   geom_tile() +   geom_text(aes(label = round(mean_slope, 4)), size = 3) +   scale_fill_gradient2(low = "#1a9850", mid = "white", high = "#d73027",                        midpoint = 0, name = "sigma slope") +   labs(     title = "Baldwin Effect phase diagram",     subtitle = "Green = canalization (sigma declining), red = flexibility favoured (sigma rising)",     x = "Grass rate (resource abundance)", y = "Seasonal amplitude (variability)"   ) +   theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

what: Experiment 2 — Is the ceiling an artefact of run length?
title: BNN uncertainty canalization and the Baldwin Effect
text: Question. baseline sigma plateaus 0.5 within 300 ticks. canalization never occurs, 600 ticks generations? found. Using canalization-favourable condition Experiment 1 (grass_rate = 0.20, seasonal_amplitude = 0.8 — cell smallest positive slope), 5 seeds 1000, 2000, 5000 ticks: slope decelerates 21-fold 1000 5000 ticks — final_sigma remains ceiling (0.5) cases. deceleration ceiling saturation, onset canalization: exploration selected strongly σ presses upper bound (0.5), ceiling reached observable slope approaches zero. ceiling raised, σ continue rising. Run length limiting factor. Baldwin Effect emerge exploration genuinely ESS competitive foraging world — fitness landscape stable peak canalization track. Sigma trajectories 1000, 2000, 5000 ticks (mean +/- SE, 5 seeds). runs hit 0.5 ceiling; slope decelerates 21-fold never reverses. ceiling saturation, canalization.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  # Use the most canalization-favourable condition from Experiment 1 # (abundant, stable: grass_rate = 0.20, seasonal_amplitude = 0) make_long_specs <- function(max_ticks) {   s <- default_specs()   s$brain_type         <- "bnn"   s$n_agents_init      <- 100L   s$grid_rows          <- 25L   s$grid_cols          <- 25L   s$grass_rate         <- 0.20   s$seasonal_amplitude <- 0.0   s$max_ticks          <- max_ticks   s }  run_lengths <- c(500L, 1000L, 2000L, 5000L) sigma_trajectories <- lapply(run_lengths, function(ticks) {   env <- run_alife(make_long_specs(ticks), verbose = FALSE)   d   <- get_run_data(env)$ticks   data.frame(t = d$t, mean_prior_sigma = d$mean_prior_sigma,              run_length = as.character(ticks)) }) traj_df <- do.call(rbind, sigma_trajectories)  ggplot(traj_df, aes(x = t, y = mean_prior_sigma, colour = run_length)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.7, alpha = 0.8) +   labs(     title    = "Does canalization emerge at longer run lengths?",     subtitle = "Stable, resource-abundant environment (grass_rate = 0.20, seasonal_amplitude = 0)",     x = "Tick", y = expression("Mean prior " * sigma),     colour   = "Run length (ticks)"   ) +   theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

title: BNN uncertainty canalization and the Baldwin Effect
text: Question. Baldwin Effect require BNN specifically, emerge within-lifetime adaptation mechanism? found. 4 brain conditions × 5 seeds × 1000 ticks (canalization-favourable environment Experiment 1: grass_rate = 0.20, seasonal_amplitude = 0.8): Three findings: RL effect sigma dynamics: BNN-BNN+RL indistinguishable slope final σ. Within-lifetime policy gradient updates alter evolutionary trajectory prior width. RL provides energy benefit 1000 ticks: ANN+RL ANN-null identical final energy (154.97 5 significant figures). REINFORCE gradient offers measurable foraging advantage 1000 ticks environment — genetic evolution ANN weights sufficient. BNN pays energy cost exploration: BNN agents end 128 energy vs ANN agents 155 — 17% metabolic penalty broad Thompson sampling. Yet sigma still selected ceiling. Agents pay explore selection still favours maintaining capacity. strongest evidence exploration ESS: even costly, preferred genetic assimilation. Left: sigma trajectories BNN-BNN+RL (seed 1). two curves indistinguishable — RL alter sigma dynamics. Right: final mean energy condition. BNN agents pay 17% energy cost relative ANN agents yet exploration ESS persists.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  # Best canalization-favourable environment from Experiment 1 base_s <- function(brain_type, rl_mode) {   s <- default_specs()   s$brain_type         <- brain_type   s$rl_mode            <- rl_mode   s$n_agents_init      <- 100L   s$grid_rows          <- 25L   s$grid_cols          <- 25L   s$grass_rate         <- 0.20   s$seasonal_amplitude <- 0.0   s$max_ticks          <- 1000L   s }  conditions <- list(   list(brain = "bnn", rl = "none",         label = "BNN only"),   list(brain = "ann", rl = "actor_critic", label = "ANN + RL"),   list(brain = "bnn", rl = "actor_critic", label = "BNN + RL"),   list(brain = "ann", rl = "none",         label = "ANN null") )  traj_list <- lapply(conditions, function(cond) {   env <- run_alife(base_s(cond$brain, cond$rl), verbose = FALSE)   d   <- get_run_data(env)$ticks   data.frame(t = d$t, mean_prior_sigma = d$mean_prior_sigma,              mean_energy = d$mean_energy, condition = cond$label) }) traj_df <- do.call(rbind, traj_list)  ggplot(traj_df, aes(x = t, y = mean_prior_sigma, colour = condition)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   labs(title = "Sigma dynamics by brain architecture",        x = "Tick", y = expression("Mean prior " * sigma),        colour = NULL) +   theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

title: BNN uncertainty canalization and the Baldwin Effect
text: Question. social transmission mechanisms (kin altruism, social learning, epigenetic inheritance) accelerate retard canalization? Existing finding. Adding kin_selection = TRUE baseline BNN run raised sigma rather lowering . interpretation: kin altruism provides energy subsidies buffer cost exploration — agents kin support can afford maintain broader priors energy risk uncertain decisions partially insured relatives. Social insurance maintains cognitive flexibility. novel interaction: social buffering retards Baldwin Effect. found. 4 modules × 2 environments × 5 seeds × 1000 ticks (40 runs). Results ordered final σ within environment: Stable abundant (grass = 0.20, seasonal = 0.8): Scarce stable (grass = 0.05, seasonal = 0.0): headline finding: epigenetic inheritance mechanism substantially reduces σ ceiling. stable abundant environment, epigenetics reduces final σ 0.500 0.342 (32% ceiling). scarce stable environment, effect even stronger: σ reaches 0.140 — 72% 0.5 ceiling 65% baseline final value 0.400. mechanism epigenetic transmission sigma : agents learn within lifetime (posterior σ contraction), offspring inherit partially-contracted priors via TEI (transgenerational epigenetic inheritance, tei_prob = 0.3). Lamarckian shortcut canalization — within-lifetime narrowing transmitted directly, bypassing slow genetic route pure Baldwinian assimilation requires. findings: - Social learning: slightly increases σ slope scarce environment (copying behaviour successful high-σ agents propagates exploration strategy), reduces final σ slightly (0.30 vs 0.40 baseline) via within-generation variance reduction - Kin selection: negligible effect stable conditions; scarce conditions, energy subsidies relatives allow high-σ exploration maintained (kin buffer cost uncertainty) — consistent earlier finding social insurance retards canalization - Prediction reversal: epigenetics predicted moderately accelerate canalization; produced largest effect observed across 4 experiments. Social learning predicted accelerate canalization; significant effect stable environment. Final mean sigma social module, faceted environment. Epigenetics (red) mechanism substantially reduces sigma — 32% ceiling stable-abundant conditions, 72% ceiling scarce-stable conditions. modules remain near 0.5 ceiling (dashed line).
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  base_s <- function(...) {   s <- default_specs()   s$brain_type         <- "bnn"   s$n_agents_init      <- 150L   # need higher density for social effects   s$grid_rows          <- 25L   s$grid_cols          <- 25L   s$grass_rate         <- 0.20   s$seasonal_amplitude <- 0.0   s$max_ticks          <- 1000L   mods <- list(...)   for (nm in names(mods)) s[[nm]] <- mods[[nm]]   s }  conditions <- list(   list(specs = base_s(),                                   label = "BNN baseline"),   list(specs = base_s(social_learning = TRUE),             label = "BNN + social learning"),   list(specs = base_s(epigenetics = TRUE, tei_prob = 0.3), label = "BNN + epigenetics"),   list(specs = base_s(kin_selection = TRUE),               label = "BNN + kin selection") )  traj_list <- lapply(conditions, function(cond) {   env <- run_alife(cond$specs, verbose = FALSE)   d   <- get_run_data(env)$ticks   data.frame(t = d$t, mean_prior_sigma = d$mean_prior_sigma,              condition = cond$label) }) traj_df <- do.call(rbind, traj_list)  ggplot(traj_df, aes(x = t, y = mean_prior_sigma, colour = condition)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   labs(title = "Social modifiers accelerate or retard canalization",        subtitle = "Kin altruism maintains exploration; social learning may accelerate assimilation",        x = "Tick", y = expression("Mean prior " * sigma), colour = NULL) +   theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

title: Body size evolution
text: models. heritable body_size trait scales metabolic costs foraging gains via allometric rules. Larger body size increases move cost also foraging efficiency. Natural selection finds optimal body size prevailing resource density. Key parameters. code: library(clade) library(ggplot2) library(patchwork)  make_s <- function(n_pred, seed) {   s <- default_specs()   s$body_size_evolution <- TRUE   s$body_size_init_mean <- 1.0   s$n_agents_init       <- 80L   s$max_agents          <- 400L   s$n_predators_init    <- as.integer(n_pred)   s$max_predators       <- as.integer(n_pred * 3L)   s$max_ticks           <- 400L   s$random_seed         <- as.integer(seed)   s }  seeds <- c(1L, 7L, 13L) no_pred_list <- lapply(seeds, function(s) {   d <- get_run_data(run_alife(make_s(0, s), verbose = FALSE))$ticks   cbind(d[, c("t", "mean_body_size")], condition = "No predators") }) pred_list <- lapply(seeds, function(s) {   d <- get_run_data(run_alife(make_s(10, s), verbose = FALSE))$ticks   cbind(d[, c("t", "mean_body_size")], condition = "Predators (10)") })  df <- do.call(rbind, c(no_pred_list, pred_list)) df_mean <- aggregate(mean_body_size ~ t + condition, data = df, FUN = mean)  ggplot(df_mean, aes(t, mean_body_size, colour = condition)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 1.0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +   scale_colour_manual(values = c("No predators" = "#4dac26",                                   "Predators (10)" = "#d01c8b")) +   labs(title = "Body size evolution: predation culls the large tail",        subtitle = "Size-dependent detectability slows Cope-direction drift",        x = "Tick", y = "Mean body size", colour = NULL) +   theme_minimal()

what: Brain size evolution and the parental provisioning hypothesis
title: Brain size evolution
text: parental provisioning hypothesis proposes parental care prerequisite brain size evolution (van Schaik et al. 2023; Griesser et al. 2023; Song et al. 2025). mechanism three components: Expensive brain hypothesis: brain tissue metabolically costly -regulated. unit brain size adds idle metabolic cost must paid every tick, regardless whether agent foraging successfully. Bootstrapping problem: large-brained newborns immediately pay metabolic surcharge forage poorly (ANN weights random birth). starve cognitive advantage can offset cost — unless parent provides exogenous energy infancy. Sensing quality: larger-brained agents perceive grass resource gradients clearly. Grass inputs neural network scaled brain_size ^ brain_size_sensing_exponent, amplifying signal nearby food cells. provides directional navigation advantage complements -cell foraging bonus compounds brain size evolves upward. key prediction: brain_size_evolution = TRUE without parental care selects large brains; parental care, larger brains can evolve. found (2026-04-15 audit, 4 seeds × 400 ticks, brain_size_cost_scale = 2.0, care_duration = 15). Full protocol: dev/audit/fidelity/brain_size.md. Direction correct (care drift > -care drift, Δ-delta = +0.009) — parental provisioning hypothesis supported qualitatively. magnitudes tiny conditions slightly decline rather diverge dramatically. prior “+0.7 care, −0.25 -care” claim reproduced parameters retracted. Flagged 🟠 passed-consistent; longer runs higher cost_scale needed sharpen contrast.

what: Example: parental care unlocks brain size evolution
title: Brain size evolution
text: Expected output: mean brain size trajectories parental-care -care conditions. parental-care condition (blue) show upward drift; -care condition (red) remain flat decline reference line (dashed). found. brain_size_cost_scale = 2.0, brain_size_init_mean = 1.1, n_agents_init = 100L, care_duration = 15L: parental-care condition (blue) showed sustained upward drift mean brain size 1.1 approximately 1.8 tick 300 — gain +0.7 units. -care condition (red) declined 1.1 approximately 0.85 tick 300 — loss −0.25 units. directions diverge within 30–50 ticks cross. parental provisioning hypothesis confirmed: care buffers infancy energy deficit long enough cognitive foraging returns dominate selection. Without care, large-brained newborns pay metabolic surcharge immediately starve neural advantage emerges, driving selection large brains. calibration note: default brain_size_cost_scale = 1.2 weak separate conditions cleanly. value cognitive foraging bonus (eat_gain × (brain_size − 1.0) = 5.0 × 0.1 = 0.5 energy/tick brain 1.1) greatly exceeds metabolic surcharge (idle_cost × cost_scale × (brain_size − 1.0) = 0.5 × 1.2 × 0.1 = 0.06 energy/tick), large brains beneficial even without parental care conditions drift upward. Setting brain_size_cost_scale ≥ 2.0 makes bootstrapping problem non-trivial produces separation seen figure. Interpretation. Brain size evolves combination three forces: metabolic cost (scales brain_size_cost_scale), cognitive foraging bonus (scales eat_gain), sensing advantage (scales brain_size_sensing_exponent). defaults, foraging bonus dominates across life span, brain size tends drift upward populations size — without parental care. parental provisioning effect strongest early life, metabolic cost paid foraging advantage materialises. Increasing brain_size_cost_scale steepens early-life deficit; increasing feeding_rate × care_duration deepens buffer. Setting brain_size_sensing_exponent = 0 disables sensing pathway entirely, allowing two effects (cost foraging bonus) isolated.
code: library(clade)  base <- default_specs() base$grid_rows     <- 20L base$grid_cols     <- 20L base$n_agents_init <- 40L base$max_agents    <- 300L base$max_ticks     <- 300L base$random_seed   <- 42L  # Brain size evolution parameters base$brain_size_evolution   <- TRUE base$brain_size_init_mean   <- 1.1   # start slightly above reference base$brain_size_mutation_sd <- 0.05 base$brain_size_cost_scale  <- 1.2   # moderate metabolic burden  # Condition 1: brain size evolution WITH parental care (care buffers infancy) s_care              <- base s_care$parental_care  <- TRUE s_care$care_duration  <- 10L s_care$feeding_rate   <- 3.0  # Condition 2: brain size evolution WITHOUT parental care (bootstrapping fails) s_no_care           <- base s_no_care$parental_care <- FALSE  env_care    <- run_alife(s_care,    verbose = FALSE) env_no_care <- run_alife(s_no_care, verbose = FALSE)  d_care    <- get_run_data(env_care)$ticks d_no_care <- get_run_data(env_no_care)$ticks  df <- rbind(   cbind(d_care[,    c("t", "mean_brain_size")], condition = "Parental care"),   cbind(d_no_care[, c("t", "mean_brain_size")], condition = "No parental care") )  ggplot(df, aes(t, mean_brain_size, colour = condition)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 1.0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +   scale_colour_manual(values = c("Parental care" = "#2196F3",                                   "No parental care" = "#F44336")) +   labs(title = "Brain size evolution: parental provisioning hypothesis",        subtitle = "Parental care bridges the bootstrapping energy deficit",        x = "Tick", y = "Mean brain size", colour = NULL) +   theme_minimal()

what: The cephalopod paradox — short lifespan selects for fast learning
title: The cephalopod paradox
text: models. Classical life-history theory predicts long-lived organisms invest heavily learning, long lifespan provides enough time recoup energetic costs large brain (payback period argument). cephalopod paradox inverts prediction: octopuses squid among shortest lifespans large-brained animal, yet express sophisticated within-lifetime learning. Liedtke & Fromhage (2019) formalised resolution: lifespan brief genetic evolution converge food-finding solutions, within-lifetime learning becomes viable mechanism adaptive behaviour. , complex_landscape = TRUE creates heterogeneous resource topology solved fixed behavioural rule, learning_rate_evolution = TRUE allows genome optimise quickly agents update neural weights experience. Key parameters. Expected output. evolved mean learning rate (recorded final tick run) highest short max_age values decline lifespan increases, long-lived agents can afford rely genetic evolution encode solutions directly. relationship need monotone short lifespans, agents little experience benefit learning.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  lifespans  <- c(20L, 50L, 100L, 200L) specs_list <- lapply(lifespans, function(ma) {   s <- default_specs()   s$max_age               <- ma   s$complex_landscape     <- TRUE   s$learning_rate_evolution <- TRUE   s$max_ticks             <- 400L   s }) names(specs_list) <- paste0("max_age_", lifespans)  results <- batch_alife(specs_list, n_cores = 4L)  lr_df <- do.call(rbind, mapply(function(env, ma) {   d <- get_run_data(env)$ticks   data.frame(     max_age       = ma,     mean_lr_final = tail(d$mean_learning_rate, 1L)   ) }, results, lifespans, SIMPLIFY = FALSE))  ggplot(lr_df, aes(x = max_age, y = mean_lr_final)) +   geom_line(colour = "#e08214", linewidth = 1) +   geom_point(size = 3, colour = "#8073ac") +   labs(     title    = "The cephalopod paradox: short lifespan selects for fast learning",     subtitle = "Evolved mean learning rate at end of run, by maximum lifespan",     x = "Maximum lifespan (ticks)", y = "Evolved mean learning rate"   ) +   theme_minimal()

what: Clutch size evolution and r/K strategy
title: Clutch size evolution
text: models. Lack clutch-size hypothesis (Lack 1947) proposes parents produce number offspring maximises number successfully raised, given resources available per offspring. Smith & Fretwell (1974) formalised quantity–quality trade-: resources scarce, producing fewer, better-provisioned offspring (K-strategy) yields higher per-offspring survival producing many poorly provisioned ones (r-strategy). scenario allows clutch size evolve freely clutch_size_min clutch_size_max two contrasting resource environments. Key parameters. Expected output. rich resources (grass_rate = 0.4), per-tick births higher large clutches sustainable. scarce resources (grass_rate = 0.05), per-tick births lower population constrained energy — consistent K end r/K continuum. two conditions diverge substantially approximately 150 ticks.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  make_s <- function(gr) {   s <- default_specs()   s$clutch_size_evolution   <- TRUE   s$clutch_size_min         <- 1L   s$clutch_size_max         <- 5L   s$clutch_size_mutation_sd <- 0.3   s$grass_rate              <- gr   s$max_ticks               <- 400L   s$random_seed             <- 3L   s }  d_rich <- get_run_data(run_alife(make_s(0.4)))$ticks d_scar <- get_run_data(run_alife(make_s(0.05)))$ticks  df <- rbind(   cbind(d_rich[, c("t", "n_births")], environment = "Rich (0.4)"),   cbind(d_scar[, c("t", "n_births")], environment = "Scarce (0.05)") )  ggplot(df, aes(t, n_births, colour = environment)) +   geom_line(alpha = 0.7) +   geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = FALSE, linewidth = 1.2) +   scale_colour_manual(     values = c("Rich (0.4)" = "#e41a1c", "Scarce (0.05)" = "#377eb8"),     name = NULL) +   labs(title = "Births per tick under contrasting resource availability",        subtitle = "Clutch size evolution enabled",        x = "Tick", y = "Births per tick") +   theme_minimal()

title: Complex landscape
text: models. three-layer habitat: ground (grass), shrubs (mid-level), canopy (top). Shrubs canopy higher energy density slower regrowth. Canopy access requires minimum wing_size (heritable trait). creates selection pressure wing-size evolution niche partitioning. scenario structurally analogous cephalopod paradox (Liedtke & Fromhage 2019): rich resource accessible agents evolved morphology exploit . Key parameters. Expected output. mean_wing_size rises time. n_canopy_agents increases wing-size evolution crosses canopy_threshold. Niche entropy (ground/shrub/canopy mix) first rises stabilises. Use tune_complex_landscape() find parameter combinations produce strong wing-size evolution CMA-ES:
code: s <- default_specs() s$complex_landscape   <- TRUE s$shrub_density       <- 0.35 s$canopy_density      <- 0.15 s$canopy_energy       <- 55.0 s$canopy_threshold    <- 0.15 s$wing_size_init_mean <- 0.08 s$max_ticks           <- 400L  env  <- run_alife(s) data <- get_run_data(env)  # Wing size evolution plot(data$ticks$t, data$ticks$mean_wing_size, type = "l",      xlab = "Tick", ylab = "Mean wing size",      main = "Wing size evolution in the forest world") tuned <- tune_complex_landscape(default_specs(), n_iterations = 80L) tuned$specs   # optimal landscape parameters

title: Cooperative breeding and public goods
text: models. cooperation_evolution = TRUE, agent carries heritable cooperation tendency trait determines contribution local public goods pool. pool benefit shared among agents neighbourhood, scaled cooperation_multiplier, contributors pay fixed cooperation_cost. implements spatially explicit Prisoner’s Dilemma: defectors (low tendency) free-ride cooperators , interactions local, spatial clustering cooperators can shield invasion defectors — result established analytically Nowak & May (1992) extended continuous strategies Hauert et al. (2002). Key parameters. Expected output. Population size rises substantially relative baseline; n_cooperation_acts positive first tick. Whether mean_cooperation_level rises falls depends interaction multiplier (group-level benefit) free-rider invasion (individual-level selection low tendency). found (2026-04-15 audit). 5-seed multi-seed run cooperation_multiplier = 2.5, 80 agents, 400 ticks. Full protocol: dev/audit/fidelity/cooperation.md. Tragedy--commons: cooperation drifts 0.014 400 ticks (small detectable), confirming free-rider invasion even group benefit raises carrying capacity. Multiplier sweep (7 levels × 3 seeds, 2026-04-15): Spearman correlation cooperation_multiplier population ρ = 1.00. Sharp transition M = 1.5 (296 agents) M = 2.0 (528 agents) — spatial Nowak-May critical regime. code: library(clade) library(ggplot2) library(patchwork)  run_cond <- function(coop_ev, seed) {   s <- default_specs()   s$cooperation_evolution  <- coop_ev   s$cooperation_multiplier <- 2.5   s$cooperation_cost       <- 1.0   s$cooperation_init_mean  <- 0.5   s$n_agents_init          <- 50L   s$max_ticks              <- 400L   s$random_seed            <- as.integer(seed)   env <- run_alife(s, verbose = FALSE)   d   <- get_run_data(env)$ticks   cbind(d[, c("t", "n_agents", "mean_cooperation_level")],         condition = if (coop_ev) "Cooperation" else "Baseline") }  seeds <- c(1L, 7L, 13L) df <- do.call(rbind, c(   lapply(seeds, function(s) run_cond(FALSE, s)),   lapply(seeds, function(s) run_cond(TRUE,  s)) )) df_mean <- aggregate(cbind(n_agents, mean_cooperation_level) ~ t + condition,                      data = df, FUN = mean)  p1 <- ggplot(df_mean, aes(t, n_agents, colour = condition)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   scale_colour_manual(values = c(Cooperation = "#1b7837", Baseline = "#d01c8b")) +   labs(title = "Public goods: population dynamics",        x = "Tick", y = "Mean n_agents (3 reps)", colour = NULL) +   theme_minimal()  p2 <- ggplot(df_mean[df_mean$condition == "Cooperation",],              aes(t, mean_cooperation_level)) +   geom_line(colour = "#1b7837", linewidth = 0.8) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +   labs(title = "Cooperation level over time (tragedy of the commons?)",        x = "Tick", y = "Mean cooperation level") +   theme_minimal()  p1 / p2

title: Group defense
text: models. Hamilton’s (1971) selfish herd hypothesis proposes aggregation reduces individual predation risk diluting attacks across larger group. single predator can attack one agent per encounter, additional group member decreases per-capita probability target. clade, group defense implemented collective damage-reduction mechanism: agents within group_defense_radius cells attacked individual jointly absorb fraction attack, reducing effective predation pressure member. Key parameters. Expected output. group_defense = TRUE condition, mean population size substantially higher baseline, survival curves diverge first predator boom. mechanism dilution: per-capita attack rate falls group size grows, creating positive feedback aggregation survival. Expected output: group defense (green) sustains larger stable population baseline (red) identical predation pressure, illustrating dilution--risk effect. found. Running 15 predators (predator_attack_strength = 60), 80 agents 30×30 grid, 400 ticks (3 replicates, seeds 41–43): group defense produced +5 agent mean population advantage (402 vs 398) reduced total deaths mean 2 per run — differences within stochastic noise. damage-reduction mechanism correctly implemented: population density ≈ 0.4 agents/cell, agent expected 9 neighbours within radius-2 Moore neighborhood, giving damage factor 1/(1 + 9 × 0.3) ≈ 0.27, theoretical 73% reduction attack damage. reason population effect negligible densities, energy-based mortality (starvation grass locally depleted) dominates predation mortality; removing 73% predation damage substantially change death rate already driven food scarcity. Hamilton’s (1971) selfish herd advantage detectable sparse founding population transitions isolation grouping — dynamic best captured starting n_agents_init = 10 comparing cumulative survival founding bottleneck phase.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  base_specs <- function() {   s <- default_specs()   s$n_predators_init <- 5L   s$n_agents_init    <- 100L   s$grid_rows        <- 30L   s$grid_cols        <- 30L   s$max_ticks        <- 400L   s }  s_no <- base_specs() s_no$group_defense <- FALSE  s_gd <- base_specs() s_gd$group_defense          <- TRUE s_gd$group_defense_radius   <- 2L s_gd$group_defense_strength <- 0.3  d_no <- get_run_data(run_alife(s_no))$ticks d_gd <- get_run_data(run_alife(s_gd))$ticks  d_no$condition <- "No group defense" d_gd$condition <- "Group defense" dat <- rbind(d_no, d_gd)  ggplot(dat, aes(x = t, y = n_agents, colour = condition)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   scale_colour_manual(     values = c("No group defense" = "#E53935", "Group defense" = "#43A047"),     name   = NULL   ) +   labs(     x     = "Tick",     y     = "Population size",     title = "Group defense reduces predation-driven extinction risk"   ) +   theme_classic(base_size = 12)

what: Kin selection and cooperative social structure
title: Kin selection
text: models. Agents can transfer energy nearby relatives via two mechanisms: Hamilton’s pedigree-based kin altruism (kin_selection = TRUE) IFfolk model (iffolk_selection = TRUE), adds parliamentary enforcement cooperative norms (Fromhage & Jennions 2019). Hamilton’s kin selection. Agents energy threshold donate fixed cost closely related Moore-neighbourhood agent relatedness r ≥ kin_altruism_r_min. Relatedness computed pedigree (parent chains): r = 0.5 parent–offspring, 0.25 siblings, 0 otherwise. operationalises Hamilton’s rule: altruistic act evolves r × benefit > cost. IFfolk parliamentary enforcement. IFfolk model extends kin selection helper_tendency trait parliament_suppression, penalises defectors (low helper_tendency) surrounded cooperators. models parliament genes (Fromhage & Jennions 2019): cooperative behaviour enforced neighbour-level suppression defection. Hamilton’s kin altruism parameters. IFfolk parliament parameters. Expected outputs. (1) Kin altruism: n_altruistic_acts positive; population size generally higher baseline; genetic diversity may slightly lower due local inbreeding. (2) IFfolk: mean_helper_tendency increases time; n_iffolk_transfers grows population size; parliament suppression accelerates convergence near 1.0. Example: Hamilton kin altruism. found (2026-04-15 audit, 60 runs). Hamilton’s rule reproduced striking clarity population level. Full protocol: dev/audit/fidelity/kin.md. Default regime (r_min = 0.25, B = 10, C = 2 → rB/C = 1.25): population rises 166 (baseline) 194 (+16.4%) 4196 altruistic acts 400 ticks. Hamilton-violating regime (B = 4, C = 10 → rB/C = 0.1): forced altruism reduces population 153 (−7.7%) — cost exceeds indirect benefit, exactly theory predicts. C × B grid (9 combinations): Spearman correlation rB/C ratio equilibrium population ρ = 0.97 — one cleanest population-level confirmations textbook prediction observed audit. (higher rB/C ratio, greater population benefit altruism. rB/C = 2.5, population rises 211 agents, +27% baseline.) Implementation note. clade’s kin altruism module evolutionary — kernel performs altruism deterministically gate (relatedness ≥ r_min energy > min_donor_energy) fires. audit tests population-level consequences Hamilton’s rule, invasion dynamics altruistic mutant. evolutionary dynamics (heritable helper_tendency), see cooperative-breeding IFfolk modules .
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2) library(patchwork)  run_one <- function(kin, seed) {   s <- default_specs()   s$kin_selection          <- kin   s$kin_altruism_r_min     <- 0.25   # help siblings (r ≥ 0.25) and closer   s$kin_altruism_cost      <- 5.0    # 5 energy units per act   s$grass_rate             <- 0.08   # scarce resources to create selection pressure   s$n_agents_init          <- 80L   s$max_agents             <- 400L   s$max_ticks              <- 300L   s$random_seed            <- as.integer(seed)   env  <- run_alife(s, verbose = FALSE)   d    <- get_run_data(env)$ticks   cbind(d[, c("t", "n_agents", "n_altruistic_acts")],         condition = if (kin) "Kin selection" else "Baseline") }  seeds <- c(1L, 7L, 13L, 19L, 25L) df <- do.call(rbind, c(   lapply(seeds, function(s) run_one(FALSE, s)),   lapply(seeds, function(s) run_one(TRUE,  s)) ))  # Average across replicates df_mean <- aggregate(cbind(n_agents, n_altruistic_acts) ~ t + condition,                      data = df, FUN = mean)  p1 <- ggplot(df_mean, aes(t, n_agents, colour = condition)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   scale_colour_manual(values = c("Kin selection" = "#2196F3",                                   "Baseline"      = "#F44336")) +   labs(title = "Kin selection: population dynamics",        x = "Tick", y = "Mean n_agents (5 reps)", colour = NULL) +   theme_minimal()  p2 <- ggplot(df_mean[df_mean$condition == "Kin selection",],              aes(t, n_altruistic_acts)) +   geom_line(colour = "#2196F3") +   labs(title = "Altruistic acts per tick",        x = "Tick", y = "n_altruistic_acts") +   theme_minimal()  p1 / p2

title: Kitchen-sink run
text: models. preceding sections isolate individual mechanisms establish clear cause--effect relationships. Real ecosystems combine many selective pressures simultaneously, interactions often non-additive. multi-module run demonstrates clade’s design genuinely compositional: combination modules can enabled together without special-casing, emergent dynamics richer sum parts.
