Genetic diversity rose steadily mean pairwise distance ~1.3 ~1.8 — directional selection predators disease maintained persistent diversity gradient. Mean body size drifted upward 1.0 ~1.05–1.1, modest consistent signal. striking feature timing: disease waves (visible pulses n_infected) preceded population dips 5–10 ticks, predator counts tracked prey density ~15-tick lag — consistent spatially structured predator-prey cycle damped niche-constructed shelters kin-directed energy subsidies. module crashed population: compositional design clade allows modules operate simultaneously without numerical instability, multi-module emergent dynamics qualitatively richer single-module run.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2) library(tidyr)  s <- default_specs() s$complex_landscape      <- TRUE s$grid_rows              <- 40L s$grid_cols              <- 40L s$n_agents_init          <- 120L s$n_predators_init       <- 5L s$disease                <- TRUE s$kin_selection          <- TRUE s$social_learning        <- TRUE s$rl_mode                <- "actor_critic" s$niche_construction     <- TRUE s$max_ticks              <- 500L  env  <- run_alife(s) tks  <- get_run_data(env)$ticks  dat_long <- pivot_longer(   tks[, c("t", "n_agents", "n_predators", "n_infected")],   cols      = c("n_agents", "n_predators", "n_infected"),   names_to  = "variable",   values_to = "value" ) dat_long$variable <- factor(   dat_long$variable,   levels = c("n_agents", "n_predators", "n_infected"),   labels = c("Prey agents", "Predators", "Infected agents") )  ggplot(dat_long, aes(x = t, y = value, colour = variable)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.7, alpha = 0.9) +   scale_colour_manual(     values = c("Prey agents"     = "#1565C0",                "Predators"       = "#B71C1C",                "Infected agents" = "#F57F17"),     name = NULL   ) +   labs(     x     = "Tick",     y     = "Count",     title = "Kitchen-sink run: predation, disease, kin selection, RL, and niche construction"   ) +   theme_classic(base_size = 12)

what: Life history: semelparous versus iteroparous strategies
title: Life history strategies
text: models. Cole’s paradox (Cole 1954) established fitness advantage iteroparity semelparity surprisingly small — one additional surviving offspring sufficient equalise two strategies simple demographic conditions. Real divergence arises differences juvenile versus adult survival, resource availability, reliability reproductive season (Roff 1992). Semelparous organisms invest reproductive resources single event die; iteroparous organisms spread reproduction across multiple seasons. scenario contrasts two life histories, holding parameters constant, reveal shapes population age structure birth-rate dynamics. Key parameters. Expected output. semelparous population shows shorter mean_age episodic bursts n_births coinciding cohort turnover. iteroparous population maintains smoother, continuous birth rate higher mean_age, population size fluctuating less dramatically generations. Expected output: semelparous population (red) shows lower mean age episodic birth bursts; iteroparous population (green) sustains higher mean age smoother birth-rate trajectory. found. Multi-seed audit (5 seeds, 80 agents, 25×25 grid, grass_rate = 0.15, 400 ticks; full protocol dev/audit/fidelity/life_history.md). three sign predictions Cole (1954) Williams (1966) hold, seed-level reproducibility 5%: striking emergent finding semelparous populations 674× stable (lower variance) iteroparous, despite 4.7× faster turnover. Tightly synchronized cohorts phase births deaths grass renewal, population fluctuations cancel ; iteroparous individuals can opportunistically survive lean periods, paradoxically decouples demography resources increases variance. Cole’s paradox — per-individual fitness gap strategies small — consistent result: equilibrium populations differ neither strategy catastrophically worse, demographic signatures differ much fitness outcomes .
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  s_sem <- default_specs() s_sem$life_history <- "semelparous" s_sem$max_ticks    <- 400L s_sem$random_seed  <- 7L  s_ite <- default_specs() s_ite$life_history <- "iteroparous" s_ite$max_ticks    <- 400L s_ite$random_seed  <- 7L  d_sem <- get_run_data(run_alife(s_sem))$ticks d_ite <- get_run_data(run_alife(s_ite))$ticks  df <- rbind(   cbind(d_sem[, c("t", "mean_age", "n_births")], strategy = "Semelparous"),   cbind(d_ite[, c("t", "mean_age", "n_births")], strategy = "Iteroparous") )  ggplot(df, aes(t, mean_age, colour = strategy)) +   geom_line() +   scale_colour_manual(values = c(Semelparous = "#e41a1c", Iteroparous = "#4daf4a"),                       name = NULL) +   labs(title = "Mean age: semelparous vs iteroparous life history",        x = "Tick", y = "Mean agent age") +   theme_minimal()

text: baseline result shows semelparous populations lower mean age episodic birth bursts, iteroparous populations sustain smoother birth rates higher mean age. go beyond: Semelparity × predation Add n_predators_init = 5L. Find senescence_rate two strategies become statistically indistinguishable mean_age n_births. Tried . Life history × seasonal amplitude also tested (50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): semelparous produced 3.5× births iteroparous (429 vs 122) ended half population (50 vs 112). Adding seasonality: semelparous + seasonal births = 429, iteroparous + seasonal births = 122 — nearly identical -seasonality result. Semelparous birth counts insensitive seasonality reproductive event triggers full clutch regardless resource state; iteroparous births scale energy therefore respond seasonal resource troughs.

title: MAP-Elites diversity search
text: Quality-Diversity (QD) algorithms search collection high-performing solutions also behaviourally diverse, rather converging single optimum (Mouret & Clune 2015). MAP-Elites divides user-defined behavioural space discrete cells fills cell best parameter configuration produces corresponding behaviour. Applied agent-based models, MAP-Elites reveals attainable combinations ecological outcomes — regions parameter space produce high genetic diversity, produce large populations, produce . search_map_elites() implements MAP-Elites algorithm clade specs. behavioural descriptor specified archive_dims, named list mapping column names get_run_data()$ticks grid bin sequences. iteration mutates randomly selected elite, runs short simulation, places result archive cell matching mean behavioural descriptor value. found. 200 MAP-Elites iterations (n_iterations = 200L, 50 agents, 20×20 grid, 200-tick runs), archive mapped behavioural dimension 1 (foraging efficiency, range 0–1.1) dimension 2 (dispersal rate, range 0–1.0). Approximately two-thirds archive cells filled — majority yellow (mean fitness ≥ 0.95), indicating high-fitness parameter configurations broadly distributed across behavioural space. low-fitness zone (purple, fitness ≤ 0.6) concentrated lower-left corner archive: parameter sets combining low foraging efficiency low dispersal systematically underperformed. identifies behavioural constraint absent single-objective optimisation run — low-dispersal agents also forage poorly compensate population growth, making region parameter space doubly penalised. upper-right portion archive (high foraging + high dispersal) sparsely populated yellow cells, suggesting high-dispersal agents also efficient foragers achievable rare — require specific combinations grass_rate, move_cost, idle_cost MAP-Elites identifies random search miss. archive provides compact summary attainable evolutionary outcomes across parameter space, identifying parameter configurations found single-objective optimisation. Dense regions archive correspond behaviourally robust parameter sets; sparse regions indicate corresponding combination outcomes rare mechanistically impossible.
code: library(clade)  specs <- default_specs() specs$n_agents_init <- 50L specs$max_ticks     <- 200L   # short runs for MAP-Elites specs$grid_rows     <- 20L specs$grid_cols     <- 20L  result <- search_map_elites(   specs_base   = specs,   archive_dims = list(     genetic_diversity = seq(0, 1,   by = 0.1),     n_agents          = seq(0, 100, by = 10)   ),   n_iterations = 200L,   objective    = "genetic_diversity",   verbose      = FALSE )  result$map   # ggplot2 heatmap of the archive

what: Mating systems: sexual versus asexual reproduction
title: Mating systems
text: models. Sexual reproduction incurs well-documented two-fold cost relative asexual reproduction — every sexual individual devotes half offspring capacity producing males — yet sex widespread across eukaryotes (Maynard Smith 1978; Williams 1975). leading explanations invoke recombination benefits sex: sexual populations generate genotypic variation per generation, providing raw material selection fluctuating parasitised environments. scenario compares haploid asexual population (ploidy = 1L) diploid sexual population (ploidy = 2L, crossover_rate = 0.1) illustrate mating system shapes trajectory genetic diversity. Key parameters. Expected output. genetic_diversity higher variable sexual condition early run, recombination continuously generates novel genotypic combinations. asexual population maintains lower diversity may converge faster local optimum — advantage stable environments — less able respond environmental change. Expected output: genetic diversity higher variable sexual (diploid) condition. asexual population converges lower diversity rapidly, illustrating recombination advantage sex. found (2026-04-15 audit, 4 seeds × 500 ticks). Full protocol: dev/audit/fidelity/mating_systems.md. stable foraging environment, theoretical recombination advantage sex reproduced — haploid asex maintains marginally higher diversity (Δ = −0.005, reversed direction small). Haploids expose mutations immediately without recessive masking, gives slightly higher standing phenotypic variation equilibrium. Maynard Smith Red-Queen prediction — sex -competes asex fluctuating selection (parasites, seasons) — tested require combining ploidy = 2 disease = TRUE seasonal_amplitude > 0 detect.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  s_asex <- default_specs() s_asex$ploidy         <- 1L s_asex$crossover_rate <- 0.0 s_asex$max_ticks      <- 400L s_asex$random_seed    <- 42L  s_sex <- default_specs() s_sex$ploidy         <- 2L s_sex$crossover_rate <- 0.1 s_sex$max_ticks      <- 400L s_sex$random_seed    <- 42L  d_asex <- get_run_data(run_alife(s_asex))$ticks d_sex  <- get_run_data(run_alife(s_sex))$ticks  df <- rbind(   cbind(d_asex[, c("t", "genetic_diversity")], system = "Asexual (haploid)"),   cbind(d_sex[,  c("t", "genetic_diversity")], system = "Sexual (diploid)") )  ggplot(df, aes(t, genetic_diversity, colour = system)) +   geom_line() +   scale_colour_manual(values = c("Asexual (haploid)" = "#377eb8",                                  "Sexual (diploid)"  = "#e41a1c"),                       name = NULL) +   labs(title = "Genetic diversity: sexual vs asexual reproduction",        x = "Tick", y = "Genetic diversity") +   theme_minimal()

title: Mimicry and toxicity
text: models. heritable toxicity trait makes prey costly attack. Predators learn signal–toxicity associations via Rescorla-Wagner rule build avoidance. models evolution warning coloration Batesian/ Müllerian mimicry (Ruxton et al. 2004): toxic prey avoided; non-toxic prey resemble toxic prey also avoided (Batesian mimicry). Key parameters. Expected output (audited 2026-04-15). Across multi-seed runs ten different parameter regimes — defaults extreme cost = 0, dose = 200, predator-saturated runs — mean_toxicity measurably evolve either direction. Maximum observed drift +0.02 (init = 0, cost, 600 ticks). See mimicry fidelity report full evidence. dynamics flat: kernel-level simplification. clade’s predator memory scalar (pred.value_estimate) updated toward prey.toxicity directly, alifeR’s R prototype uses signal vector updated toward prey.signal. scalar simplification removes signal-specific learning — predators recognise warning patterns, general aversion level — mechanism Bates (1862) Müller (1879) actually described. Predators therefore rarely learn avoid toxic prey (max 9 avoidance events / 600 ticks extreme settings), without selective avoidance, toxic prey gain measurable survival advantage. Restoring signal-vector predator memory 0.4.0 backlog.
code: s <- default_specs() s$mimicry            <- TRUE s$n_predators_init   <- 5L s$toxicity_init_mean <- 0.1 s$max_ticks          <- 300L  env  <- run_alife(s) data <- get_run_data(env)

what: Calibrated regime (formerly CMA-ES discovered — claim retracted)
title: Mimicry and toxicity
text: Expected output: mean toxicity rises predator pressure; avoided attacks increase predators learn avoid toxic prey; toxic attack rate declines. found (2026-04-15 audit). Across 5 seeds four regimes spanning n_predators_init ∈ {0, 8, 15}, toxin_dose ∈ {10, 30, 60, 100, 200}, toxicity_cost ∈ {0.0, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0}, mean toxicity remained essentially locked initial value (every regime within ±0.02 toxicity_init_mean). Cumulative counters across 600 ticks: 15–67 toxic attacks vs 700+ total kills (2.5–10% rate, much lower ~50% expected predators attacked toxic non-toxic prey indiscriminately population proportions); 0–9 avoidance events total (avoidance learning essentially never fires). Diagnosis. mechanism wired correctly per kernel’s simplified semantics: toxic prey damage attacking predators, predator memory updates toxic encounters, avoidance can trigger threshold. scalar-memory simplification relative alifeR (see “dynamics flat” ) prevents signal-specific learning loop produces textbook aposematism. Consequence: textbook Bates/Müller dynamics reproduced tested parameter setting kernel ports alifeR’s vector-signal predator memory. Batesian mode (0.3.0 new). Set s$batesian_mimicry <- TRUE let palatable mimics (toxicity = 0) share learned aversion (Bates 1862). Predator-betrayal decay regulates mimic frequency.

title: Module comparison
text: models. Biological systems shaped multiple interacting selective pressures, individual contributions difficult disentangle nature. scenario applies one-module---time factorial design: condition activates exactly one module shared baseline, three summary statistics — mean genetic diversity, mean population size, mean energy — recorded across full run. Comparing modules isolation combining allows module’s unique contribution identified; -condition reveals emergent interactions predicted single-module results alone. Key parameters. Expected output. Modules imposing strong directional selection — predators disease — tend increase genetic diversity maintaining selective gradient; modules smooth energy acquisition — niche construction, social learning — may reduce within-population variance. -condition typically reveals non-additive interactions: cooperation kin selection can partially compensate disease mortality, niche construction can buffer energetic cost predation evasion. Expected output: horizontal bar chart mean genetic diversity across 14 conditions. Conditions imposing strong directional selection (predators, disease, mimicry) appear near top; -condition reveals emergent multi-module interactions. found. dashboard shows -condition tick 500 500-tick run (120 agents, 30×30 grid, 5 predators, disease, kin selection, social learning, RL, niche construction, body size evolution active simultaneously): 362 agents survived, 758 total deaths, mean body size evolved 1.0 approximately 1.3. Genetic diversity rose steadily throughout run; lifespan distribution shows majority agents dying age, starvation — indicating population remained energetically healthy despite combined selective load. Across single-module conditions, predators disease produce strongest directional selection (highest mean genetic diversity), social learning niche construction smooth energy gradients reduce within-population variance. -condition simply sum effects: cooperation kin selection partially compensate disease mortality, niche-constructed shelters buffer energetic cost predator evasion, producing population larger diverse predator-disease-conditions.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  make_specs <- function(...) {   s <- default_specs()   s$max_ticks <- 300L   args <- list(...)   for (nm in names(args)) s[[nm]] <- args[[nm]]   s }  specs_list <- list(   baseline    = make_specs(),   predators   = make_specs(n_predators_init = 5L),   disease     = make_specs(disease = TRUE),   kin         = make_specs(kin_selection = TRUE),   niche       = make_specs(niche_construction = TRUE),   rl          = make_specs(rl_mode = "actor_critic"),   social      = make_specs(social_learning = TRUE),   body_size   = make_specs(body_size_evolution = TRUE),   dispersal   = make_specs(dispersal_evolution = TRUE),   care        = make_specs(parental_care = TRUE),   mimicry     = make_specs(mimicry = TRUE, n_predators_init = 5L),   landscape   = make_specs(complex_landscape = TRUE),   cooperation = make_specs(cooperation_evolution = TRUE),   all_on      = make_specs(     n_predators_init      = 5L,     disease               = TRUE,     kin_selection         = TRUE,     niche_construction    = TRUE,     rl_mode               = "actor_critic",     social_learning       = TRUE,     body_size_evolution   = TRUE,     dispersal_evolution   = TRUE,     cooperation_evolution = TRUE   ) )  results <- batch_alife(specs_list, n_cores = 4L)  summary_df <- do.call(rbind, mapply(function(env, nm) {   d <- get_run_data(env)$ticks   data.frame(     condition      = nm,     mean_diversity = mean(d$genetic_diversity, na.rm = TRUE),     mean_pop       = mean(d$n_agents,          na.rm = TRUE),     mean_energy    = mean(d$mean_energy,       na.rm = TRUE)   ) }, results, names(results), SIMPLIFY = FALSE))  ggplot(summary_df,        aes(x = reorder(condition, mean_diversity), y = mean_diversity)) +   geom_col(fill = "#4dac26") +   coord_flip() +   labs(     title = "Mean genetic diversity by module condition",     x     = NULL,     y     = "Mean genetic diversity"   ) +   theme_minimal()

title: Pace-of-life syndromes
text: models. Pace--life syndromes (Réale et al. 2010) describe co-variation life-history traits, behaviour, physiology along slow–fast continuum. Fast-living organisms high metabolic rates, reproduce early often, die young; slow-living organisms invest maintenance, defer reproduction, achieve longer lifespans. Stearns (1992) formalised consequence age-specific mortality schedule: high extrinsic mortality selects early reproduction fast metabolism. scenario fixes metabolic rate across three values isolate direct effect metabolic pace age–reproduction–energy trade-. Key parameters. Expected output. Fast-pace agents (metabolic_rate_init_mean = 2.0) exhibit higher n_births per tick, lower mean_age, higher energy throughput, populations volatile. Slow-pace agents (metabolic_rate_init_mean = 0.5) show lower birth rate, higher mean_age, stable mean_energy. Fast agents dominate productive environments; slow agents persist reliably resources lean. Audit caveat (2026-04-15). 5-level sweep (rates 0.5 3.0), metrics flat: mean_age ≈ 98, n_agents ≈ 258, births ≈ 1.44, variance ≈ 1300, regardless metabolic_rate. Cause: clade’s always-max_age = 200 dominates age schedule — agents die cap metabolic-rate differences can accumulate. Enabling Gompertz senescence removing age cap needed reproduce Réale et al. pace--life divergence. See dev/audit/fidelity/pace_of_life.md.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  make_run <- function(rate, seed = 99L) {   s <- default_specs()   s$metabolic_rate_init_mean <- rate   s$metabolic_rate_evolution <- FALSE   s$max_ticks                <- 400L   s$random_seed              <- seed   get_run_data(run_alife(s))$ticks }  d_slow     <- make_run(0.5) d_baseline <- make_run(1.0) d_fast     <- make_run(2.0)  df <- rbind(   cbind(d_slow[,     c("t", "mean_age", "n_births")], pace = "Slow (0.5)"),   cbind(d_baseline[, c("t", "mean_age", "n_births")], pace = "Baseline (1.0)"),   cbind(d_fast[,     c("t", "mean_age", "n_births")], pace = "Fast (2.0)") )  ggplot(df, aes(t, mean_age, colour = pace)) +   geom_line() +   scale_colour_manual(     values = c("Slow (0.5)" = "#377eb8", "Baseline (1.0)" = "#4daf4a",                "Fast (2.0)" = "#e41a1c"),     name = NULL) +   labs(title = "Pace-of-life: mean age across metabolic rates",        x = "Tick", y = "Mean agent age") +   theme_minimal()

code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  make_s <- function(fi) {   s <- default_specs()   s$parental_care                 <- TRUE   s$parental_investment_evolution <- TRUE   s$female_investment             <- fi   s$male_repro_cost               <- 0.3   s$max_ticks                     <- 400L   s$random_seed                   <- 11L   s }  d_hi <- get_run_data(run_alife(make_s(0.9)))$ticks d_eq <- get_run_data(run_alife(make_s(0.5)))$ticks  df <- rbind(   cbind(d_hi[, c("t", "n_births", "n_juveniles")],         condition = "High maternal (0.9)"),   cbind(d_eq[, c("t", "n_births", "n_juveniles")],         condition = "Equal (0.5)") )  ggplot(df, aes(t, n_births, colour = condition)) +   geom_line(alpha = 0.7) +   geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = FALSE, linewidth = 1.2) +   scale_colour_manual(     values = c("High maternal (0.9)" = "#e41a1c", "Equal (0.5)" = "#377eb8"),     name = NULL) +   labs(title = "Births per tick by parental investment allocation",        subtitle = "High maternal investment → fewer but better-provisioned offspring",        x = "Tick", y = "Births per tick") +   theme_minimal()

title: Population genetics and heritability
text: models. central quantity quantitative genetics narrow-sense heritability, h² — proportion phenotypic variance trait attributable additive genetic effects (Falconer & Mackay 1996; Lynch & Walsh 1998). trait respond natural selection unless h² > 0. estimate_heritability() function performs parent-offspring regression pedigree records accumulated simulation: offspring trait values regressed mid-parent values, slope estimates h². body_size_evolution = TRUE, body size polygenic trait encoded neural genome subject mutation selection, making suitable target heritability analysis within single run. Running body_size_evolution = TRUE, 100 agents, 200 ticks (seed 42): body size showed lag-1 autocorrelation 0.985, high heritability proxy consistent strong parent–offspring resemblance. Mean body size evolved 1.001 1.033 (+3.3%) 200 ticks, trait SD = 0.086 across run. high autocorrelation reflects mean_body_size changes smoothly ticks (one generation turns per ticks), parent–offspring regression slope fall range 0.5–0.8 computed directly pedigree records. get_run_data() returns tick-level summary; construct actual parent–offspring regression, collect agent’s body_size parent_id death env$deaths, match offspring parent body size, use lm(offspring_size ~ parent_size).
code: library(clade)  s <- default_specs() s$body_size_evolution <- TRUE s$max_ticks           <- 500L  env  <- run_alife(s) data <- get_run_data(env)  # `estimate_heritability()` returns a lag-1 autocorrelation proxy for # narrow-sense heritability on the population mean trajectory. For the # parent-offspring regression itself, use `heritability_estimate()` on # `env$deaths`, which requires a trait column logged at death. h2_proxy <- estimate_heritability(data, trait = "body_size") cat("Heritability proxy (lag-1 autocorrelation) for body_size:",     round(h2_proxy$h2, 3), "\n") cat("n =", h2_proxy$n, "  method =", h2_proxy$method, "\n")  # Mean trajectory — the quantity the proxy is computed on. plot(data$ticks$t, data$ticks$mean_body_size, type = "l",      xlab = "Tick", ylab = "Mean body size",      main = "Body size evolution (proxy for heritable response)")

code: library(clade) library(ggplot2) library(patchwork)  make_s <- function(n_pred) {   s <- default_specs()   s$n_agents_init    <- 80L   s$n_predators_init <- n_pred   s$max_ticks        <- 500L   s }  results <- batch_alife(   list(no_predators = make_s(0L), predators = make_s(10L)),   n_cores = 2L )  plot_df <- do.call(rbind, mapply(function(env, nm) {   d <- get_run_data(env)$ticks   data.frame(     t                = d$t,     genetic_diversity = d$genetic_diversity,     mean_energy      = d$mean_energy,     condition        = nm   ) }, results, names(results), SIMPLIFY = FALSE))  p1 <- ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = t, y = genetic_diversity, colour = condition)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.7) +   scale_colour_manual(values = c(no_predators = "#4dac26",                                  predators    = "#d01c8b")) +   labs(title = "Predation and neural genome diversity",        x = "Tick", y = "Genetic diversity", colour = NULL) +   theme_minimal()  p2 <- ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = t, y = mean_energy, colour = condition)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.7) +   scale_colour_manual(values = c(no_predators = "#4dac26",                                  predators    = "#d01c8b")) +   labs(x = "Tick", y = "Mean energy", colour = NULL) +   theme_minimal()  p1 / p2

title: Predator-prey dynamics
text: models. Predator-prey coevolution one oldest empirically grounded problems population biology. classical Lotka-Volterra equations predict sustained sinusoidal oscillations predator abundance tracks prey abundance quarter-cycle lag (Lotka 1925; Volterra 1926). Crucially, mean-field prediction assumes fixed, mass-action predators. clade, predators carry evolving neural-network genome — learn, mutate, adapt across generations. single change enough break textbook LV signature, specific well-documented way (see “found” ). Key parameters. Expected output (evolutionary ABM, mean-field LV). Watch n_agents (prey) n_predators time calibrated parameters . Two distinct phases emerge: Bootstrap (t ≈ 0–30). Initial predators random neural genomes hunt poorly. Prey climb rapidly toward grass-imposed carrying capacity. Predators survive long enough reproduce pass slightly-better--random hunting brains. Arms-race equilibrium (t ≈ 30+). Predator brains evolve toward efficient pursuit; prey brains evolve toward avoidance. Predators saturate cap (predator_max_agents) stay even prey sparse — efficient hunters extract enough food non-zero prey density maintain cap-limited population. Prey continue oscillate (driven grass renewal × predation pressure), classic quarter-cycle predator lag textbook LV muted: predator decline phase LV requires suppressed ongoing pursuit-trait evolution. documented behaviour evolutionary predator-prey ABMs (see predator-prey fidelity report cross-reference alifeR prototype, contains explanation §12 showcase vignette). recover textbook sustained sinusoidal LV, need fixed-policy predator (yet exposed spec) non-toroidal patchy spatial structure (Huffaker 1958; future scenario). Calibrated predator-prey regime (seed 42, 1000 ticks). Predators bootstrap 10 cap 100 tick ~30 stay remainder — arms-race equilibrium. Prey climb peak ~460 tick 200, crash ~250 predator pressure builds, continue oscillating amplitude ±30 around mean ~300. classic LV quarter-cycle predator lag muted evolved predators track prey efficiently regardless density. found. calibrated parameters , seed 42, 1000 ticks: predators bootstrap 10 cap 100 tick ~30 remain saturated rest run — arms-race equilibrium, kernel limit hit (multi-seed audit confirmed runs predator_max_agents = 800 show identical saturation behaviour ~6–12 individuals energy granted kills). Prey climb 150 466 tick 200, crash 245 ticks 200–250 cap-limited predators consume faster grass renewal can replace , continue oscillating around ~300 amplitude ±30. Across 10 seeds prey oscillation score (magnitude negative autocorrelation lag-20--lag-100 range tick 100) 0.39 ± 0.14 — reproducible cycle. Predator oscillation score ≈ 0 design: evolved, track prey efficiently never enter LV decline phase. expected signature evolutionary predator-prey ABM, matches alifeR R prototype’s documented behaviour (see alifeR/vignettes/showcase.Rmd §12 — package author’s explanation evolving predators suppress textbook LV oscillations). Lotka 1925’s mean-field sustained sinusoidal result, longer claim . Full cross-reference table audit protocol: dev/audit/fidelity/predator_prey.md.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  s <- default_specs() s$n_predators_init          <- 10L s$n_agents_init             <- 100L s$grid_rows                 <- 30L s$grid_cols                 <- 30L s$predator_energy_gain      <- 30 s$predator_min_repro_energy <- 50 s$predator_max_agents       <- 100L s$grass_rate                <- 0.20 s$max_ticks                 <- 1000L s$random_seed               <- 42L  env  <- run_alife(s) data <- get_run_data(env)  ggplot(data$ticks, aes(x = t)) +   geom_line(aes(y = n_agents,    colour = "Prey"),     linewidth = 0.8) +   geom_line(aes(y = n_predators, colour = "Predators"), linewidth = 0.8) +   scale_colour_manual(     values = c("Prey" = "#2196F3", "Predators" = "#F44336"),     name   = NULL   ) +   labs(     x     = "Tick",     y     = "Population size",     title = "Evolutionary predator-prey: prey oscillate, predators arms-race to cap"   ) +   theme_classic(base_size = 12)

title: Predator-prey dynamics

Currently kernel always evolves predator brains, scenario can’t yet tested directly. Can spatial-refugia LV recovered? Worth dedicated s-spatial-refugia scenario. Prey trait evolution predation. Turn body_size_evolution = TRUE. predation, larger prey selected (harder kill) smaller (faster maturation)? Run 10 seeds 0, 5, 15 predators 1000+ ticks compare final mean_body_size.

title: Within-lifetime reinforcement learning

Key parameters. Expected output. Agents RL enabled adapt within lifetime local resource distribution. learning_rate_evolution = TRUE, evolved learning rate reflects environmental uncertainty: noisy environments favour lower rates; stable environments favour higher rates. Expected output: agents within-lifetime RL adapt local resource distribution within lifetime. Baldwin Effect visible BNN prior sigma narrows generations. found. Running 3 replicates rl_mode = "actor_critic" vs "none", 80 agents, 25×25 grid, grass_rate = 0.15, 400 ticks (seeds 41–43): mean population 208 (RL) vs 207 (RL); mean energy 124.8 vs 124.85. BNN prior sigma rose ~0.30 early ~0.50 late conditions — opposite expected Baldwin Effect canalization. learning_rate = 0.01 rl_update_freq = 5, REINFORCE gradient step small relative BNN’s per-tick weight resampling broad sigma prior. copied delta diluted next update, preventing sustained policy improvement. Baldwin Effect (sigma narrowing genetic assimilation) predicted require: () runs longer 400 ticks allow genetic fixation; (ii) narrower initial sigma heritable selection. dedicated BNN canalization scenario (see ) uses 500-tick run likely reveal sigma trajectory.

title: Scavenging and carrion dynamics

Carrion decays tick rate carrion_decay_rate can consumed agent moves onto cell, yielding carrion_eat_gain energy units. Key parameters. code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  base_specs <- function() {   s <- default_specs()   s$n_agents_init <- 100L   s$grid_rows     <- 30L   s$grid_cols     <- 30L   s$grass_rate    <- 0.15   s$max_ticks     <- 400L   s }  s_no <- base_specs(); s_no$scavenging <- FALSE s_sc <- base_specs() s_sc$scavenging        <- TRUE s_sc$carrion_fraction  <- 0.5 s_sc$carrion_decay_rate <- 0.1 s_sc$carrion_eat_gain  <- 3.0  d_no <- get_run_data(run_alife(s_no))$ticks d_sc <- get_run_data(run_alife(s_sc))$ticks  d_no$condition <- "No scavenging" d_sc$condition <- "Scavenging" dat <- rbind(d_no, d_sc)  ggplot(dat, aes(x = t, y = mean_energy, colour = condition)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   scale_colour_manual(     values = c("No scavenging" = "#E53935", "Scavenging" = "#FB8C00"),     name   = NULL   ) +   labs(     x     = "Tick",     y     = "Mean agent energy",     title = "Scavenging sustains energy budgets under resource scarcity"   ) +   theme_classic(base_size = 12)

code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  make_specs <- function(carrion_tp = 0.0) {   s <- default_specs()   s$n_agents_init           <- 200L   s$grid_rows               <- 25L   s$grid_cols               <- 25L   s$grass_rate              <- 0.08   s$max_ticks               <- 300L   s$scavenging              <- TRUE   s$carrion_fraction        <- 0.5   s$carrion_decay_rate      <- 0.1   s$disease                 <- TRUE   s$transmission_prob       <- 0.15   s$disease_seed_prob       <- 0.03   s$carrion_transmission_prob <- carrion_tp   s }  d_safe <- get_run_data(run_alife(make_specs(0.0)))$ticks d_risk <- get_run_data(run_alife(make_specs(0.3)))$ticks  d_safe$condition <- "Carrion safe (prob = 0)" d_risk$condition <- "Carrion risky (prob = 0.3)" dat <- rbind(d_safe, d_risk)  ggplot(dat, aes(x = t, y = n_new_infections, colour = condition)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.7, alpha = 0.8) +   scale_colour_manual(     values = c("Carrion safe (prob = 0)" = "#43A047",                "Carrion risky (prob = 0.3)" = "#E53935"),     name = NULL   ) +   labs(     x     = "Tick",     y     = "New infections per tick",     title = "Carrion as pathogen reservoir amplifies epidemics"   ) +   theme_classic(base_size = 12)

title: Seasonal dynamics
text: models. Seasonality imposes periodic variation resource availability mortality risk drives boom-bust dynamics across wide range taxa (Boyce 1979). clade, grass productivity oscillates sinusoidally period season_length amplitude seasonal_amplitude. winter phases, grass regrowth slows per-tick death probability (winter_death_prob) adds additional mortality pressure. result structured environment agents must survive periods scarcity reproduce periods abundance — minimal model temperate seasonality. Key parameters. Expected output. Population size (n_agents) track grass coverage short lag, producing recurrent crashes winter recoveries spring. Multiple complete cycles visible 500 ticks given season_length = 100. found. seasonal_amplitude = 0.6, winter_death_prob = 0.01, 100 agents 30×30 grid, 500 ticks (seed 42): population oscillated 13 223 agents (mean 80). Grass coverage tracked 100-tick sinusoidal cycle clearly — minimum ticks 75, 175, 275 (winter phase) maximum 25, 125, 225 (summer). Population lagged grass approximately 15–20 ticks. aggressive parameters (seasonal_amplitude = 0.8, winter_death_prob = 0.05), population crashed 0 ticks mean dropped 35 — parameter calibration matters substantially observing smooth seasonal cycles rather extinction dynamics. Expected output: population size (blue) tracks scaled grass productivity (green) short lag, producing recurrent winter crashes spring recoveries approximately five complete cycles.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2) library(tidyr)  s <- default_specs() s$seasonal_amplitude <- 0.8 s$season_length      <- 100L s$winter_death_prob  <- 0.05 s$n_agents_init      <- 100L s$grid_rows          <- 30L s$grid_cols          <- 30L s$max_ticks          <- 500L  env  <- run_alife(s) tks  <- get_run_data(env)$ticks  tks$grass_scaled <- tks$grass_coverage /   max(tks$grass_coverage, na.rm = TRUE) *   max(tks$n_agents,       na.rm = TRUE)  dat_long <- pivot_longer(   tks[, c("t", "n_agents", "grass_scaled")],   cols      = c("n_agents", "grass_scaled"),   names_to  = "variable",   values_to = "value" ) dat_long$variable <- ifelse(dat_long$variable == "n_agents",                             "Population size", "Grass coverage (scaled)")  ggplot(dat_long, aes(x = t, y = value, colour = variable)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   scale_colour_manual(     values = c("Population size" = "#1565C0",                "Grass coverage (scaled)" = "#2E7D32"),     name = NULL   ) +   labs(     x     = "Tick",     y     = "Value (agents / scaled grass)",     title = "Seasonal boom-bust dynamics"   ) +   theme_classic(base_size = 12)

Genetic diversity peaked moderate amplitude (gd = 0.188 amp = 0.3) remained stable higher amplitudes. extreme amplitude (amp = 0.9) drove grass coverage low 7% populations persisted — agents buffer seasonal troughs within generation rather tracking demographically 200-tick timescales. Tried . Adding parental_care = TRUE alongside seasonality (50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): final population identical (n = 111 vs 111), genetic diversity marginally increased care (gd = 0.191 vs 0.188). brain-size cognitive buffering hypothesis requires longer runs: 200 ticks, seasonal amplitude (0.3–0.9) produced measurable increase evolved brain size relative -seasonality baseline. 5-seed cross-module gallery found 0.6% brain-size difference stable seasonal environments.

what: Signals and mate choice: sexual selection
title: Signals and mate choice
text: models. Sexual selection can produce elaborate traits reduce survival increase mating success. Two contrasting mechanisms proposed. Zahavi’s (1975) handicap principle requires signals condition-dependent costly maintain, high-quality individuals can afford elaborate signals; signal magnitude honest indicator genetic quality. Fisher’s (1930) runaway process operates genetic correlation signal elaboration preference: females prefer trait, sons choosy females inherit trait daughters inherit preference, driving fixation. scenario enables multi-dimensional signals female preference, allowing mechanisms operate, tracks whether signal magnitude remains correlated mean_energy (consistent honest signalling) becomes decoupled (consistent runaway dynamics). Key parameters. Expected output. mean_signal_magnitude increases time sexual selection drives signal elaboration. signal_cost sufficient maintain honesty, mean_signal_magnitude correlates positively mean_energy: agents better condition carry elaborate signals. weak zero signal_cost allows decoupling signal condition, consistent runaway dynamics.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  s <- default_specs() s$signal_dims          <- 3L s$signal_cost          <- 0.05 s$mate_choice_mode     <- "preference" s$mate_choice_strength <- 0.7 s$max_ticks            <- 400L s$random_seed          <- 21L  env  <- run_alife(s) data <- get_run_data(env)  p1 <- ggplot(data$ticks, aes(t, mean_signal_magnitude)) +   geom_line(colour = "#984ea3") +   labs(title = "Signal elaboration under sexual selection",        x = "Tick", y = "Mean signal magnitude") +   theme_minimal()  p2 <- ggplot(data$ticks, aes(mean_energy, mean_signal_magnitude)) +   geom_point(alpha = 0.4, size = 0.8, colour = "#984ea3") +   geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, colour = "#333333", linewidth = 0.7) +   labs(title = "Signal magnitude vs mean energy (honest signalling check)",        x = "Mean energy", y = "Mean signal magnitude") +   theme_minimal()  print(p1) print(p2)

title: Signals and mate choice
text: Running Phase 7 auto-calibration (dev/audit/calibration/) scenario’s parameter subspace discovered following regime, produces fitness improvement 6.5x defaults . See dev/audit/calibration/RESULTS.md full CMA-ES results. Expected output: mean signal magnitude rises time sexual selection drives elaboration. positive correlation signal magnitude mean energy indicates handicap mechanism maintaining signal honesty. found (2026-04-15 audit). 5-seed multi-seed run signal_dims = 3, signal_cost = 0.05, mate_choice_mode = "preference", 120 agents, 500 ticks. Full protocol: dev/audit/fidelity/signals.md. Zahavi honesty test. Within-run Spearman correlation mean_energy mean_signal_magnitude averaged ρ = 0.25 across 5 seeds — positive consistent Zahavi handicap (better-conditioned agents carry elaborate signals). Cost sweep (3 seeds × 5 values signal_cost): signal magnitude flat ~1.0 across entire range 0.0 0.20. Cost affect population size (247 → 197), equilibrium signal magnitude. clade-specific finding: drift determines signal stationary distribution, cost manifests demographic attrition rather signal reduction. strict textbook Zahavi dynamics (cost reduces signal magnitude) kernel extension heritable individual-level preferences needed.
code: # Parameter overrides discovered by CMA-ES (see dev/audit/calibration/): s <- default_specs() s$signal_cost                    <- 0.0681 s$signal_drift_sd                <- 0.0533 # env <- run_alife(s)   # uncomment to run the calibrated regime

title: Signals and mate choice
text: baseline result shows signal elaboration sexual selection, signal magnitude correlating positively mean energy signal_cost maintains honesty. go beyond: Signal × brain size Add brain_size_evolution = TRUE. Larger-brained agents better sensory processing; brain size evolve faster signals provide additional information mate quality mate_choice_mode = "preference"? Compare mean_brain_size trajectories without mate choice enabled. Tried . signal_dims = 2, signal_cost = 0.1, mate_choice_mode = "preference", 60 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42: signal magnitude remained 0.000 -brain brain-evolution conditions — signal evolution initiate within 200 ticks default parameters. Brain size brain_size_evolution = TRUE reached 1.013 vs fixed 1.000 baseline. observe signal elaboration, longer runs (≥ 500 ticks) higher initial signal_init_mean needed move signal magnitude zero first selection event. Signal honesty resource stress Run grass_rate = 0.05. mean condition uniformly low, handicap principle predicts signal honesty breaks high-quality individuals afford distinguish . correlation mean_signal_magnitude mean_energy weaken scarcity compared rich-resource baseline? Tried . Across three signal dimension settings (signal_dims = 1, 2, 4; 50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): mean signal remained 0 conditions. Signal evolution requires signal_evolution_drift = TRUE generate initial mutational variation default value 0. Without drift, signal trait heritable variation selection act. signal_evolution_drift = TRUE signal_drift_sd = 0.05, signals begin diverge within 100 ticks; adding resource stress allows honest-signal correlation measured. Signal × cooperation Add cooperation_evolution = TRUE. Can heritable cooperation tendency serve honest signal genetic quality, substituting costly ornamental signals? cooperators higher mean energy (public goods), mean_signal_magnitude decline cooperation available alternative quality indicator? Tried . Three conditions tested (80 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): signals + mimicry showed mean toxicity = 0.018, avoided attacks = 0, n = 96; mimicry alone showed toxicity = 0.023, n = 100; signals alone showed toxicity = 0, n = 93. Adding signals slightly reduced evolved toxicity (0.018 vs 0.023 without signals), suggesting mild trade-two costly traits. Avoided attacks remained 0 conditions, confirming predator avoidance learning requires longer runs accumulate sufficient signal-toxicity correlations Batesian mimicry dynamics emerge.

title: Social learning
text: models. Agents can copy output-layer weights successful neighbours (energy threshold). model vertical- horizontal-cultural transmission: information actions rewarding propagates socially, accelerating adaptation relative genetic evolution alone (Henrich & McElreath 2003). Key parameters. Expected output. Mean energy may higher baseline run identical specs, particularly novel changing environments. benefit visible mutation_sd low (little genetic variation) successful behaviours exist population. Expected output: mean energy genetic diversity maintained higher levels baseline run, successful foraging behaviours propagate social network. found. effect social learning depends critically brain type. brain_type = "ann" (deterministic weights), social learning produced +4.5% mean population advantage (mean 158 vs 151, 2 replicates, 300 ticks): copied output-layer weights persist network benefit subsequent actions. brain_type = "bnn" (default stochastic weights), advantage zero (mean 183 conditions): BNN agents resample weights prior distribution tick, diluting policy copied social model can influence behaviour. reveals important constraint cultural transmission model: social learning operates output-layer weights, BNN agents retain weights ticks. interaction social_learning = TRUE + rl_mode = "actor_critic" predicted restore social learning benefits RL updates (applied BNN sample) reinforce copied policy within agent’s lifetime next sampling step.
code: s <- default_specs() s$social_learning      <- TRUE s$social_learning_freq <- 10L s$n_agents_init        <- 150L   # social learning requires sufficient density s$max_ticks            <- 300L  env  <- run_alife(s) data <- get_run_data(env)

title: Social learning
text: baseline result shows mean energy genetic diversity maintained higher levels baseline, successful foraging behaviours propagate socially. go beyond: Social learning × stress hypermutation Add stress_hypermutation = TRUE. Social copying propagates existing strategies; hypermutation generates novel ones. interact synergistically (novel strategies discovered hypermutation spread faster socially) antagonistically (social copying incumbent strategies disrupted hypermutation noise)? Tried . social_learning = TRUE, grass_rate = 0.05, 60 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42: without hypermutation — mean energy = 109.4, diversity = 0.1821, final n = 101. stress_hypermutation = TRUE — mean energy = 109.8, diversity = 0.1805, final n = 104. combination mildly synergistic population size (+3%) energy, diversity slightly lower. Hypermutation generates novel strategies social learning rapidly spreads, reducing range strategies circulation — mild homogenisation effect rather expected diversity amplification. Social learning × speciation Add speciation = TRUE. social learning slow speciation homogenising behaviours across incipient species (cultural gene flow), accelerate allowing rapid behavioural niche partitioning without genetic change? Watch whether n_species grows faster slower social learning enabled. Tried . Four social_learning_freq values (1, 5, 10, 20; 50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): sl_events = 0 conditions. Social learning events logged 0 throughout — sl_events column suggests social copying threshold triggered 50-agent density. Population size (99–110) genetic diversity (0.184–0.185) nearly identical regardless learning frequency. social learning module appears require either larger populations closer-proximity agents trigger copying events reliably. Learning radius × genetic diversity Vary social_learning_radius 1 10 across batch_alife(). Theory predicts large learning radius homogenises behaviour reduces genetic diversity spreading successful strategies. genetic diversity vs radius relationship monotone, small radius also reduce diversity (insufficient copying spread beneficial innovations)? Tried . Social learning + RL combined (50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): gd = 0.190, sl_events = 0, vs RL : gd = 0.190. Neither combination social learning alone showed active sl_events. RL social learning appear operate independently population scale — social learning copying RL-updated weights competing RL exploration. zero sl_events limitation means genetic diversity comparison reflects RL dynamics , predicted social-genetic interaction.

title: Speciation and genetic divergence
text: models. Speciation ecological character displacement occurs disruptive selection resource-use trait, combined assortative mating among similar phenotypes, drives single population split reproductively isolated lineages (Dieckmann & Doebeli 1999). genomes diverge beyond threshold, gene flow incipient species reduced, accelerating divergence. Coyne & Orr (2004) reviewed extensive empirical evidence reproductive isolation accumulates approximately linearly genetic distance. scenario enables speciation module, tracks number distinct genetic clusters (n_species) detected regular intervals, illustrates within-species diversity compares -species divergence course run. Key parameters. Expected output. n_species starts 1 rises 2–4 accumulated genome divergence exceeds isolation_threshold geographically ecologically separated subpopulations. Within-cluster genetic diversity remains lower -cluster diversity isolation established. timing first split depends population size, mutation rate, stringency isolation_threshold.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  # Quick-start settings: lower threshold and higher mutation allow speciation # within ~400 ticks. The default isolation_threshold = 0.5 requires >1,000 # ticks to accumulate sufficient genome divergence. s <- default_specs() s$speciation                  <- TRUE s$isolation_threshold         <- 0.15   # lower = easier speciation s$mutation_sd                 <- 0.15   # higher = faster divergence s$speciation_cluster_interval <- 10L s$max_ticks                   <- 1000L s$random_seed                 <- 55L  env  <- run_alife(s) data <- get_run_data(env)  ggplot(data$ticks, aes(t, n_species)) +   geom_step(colour = "#ff7f00", linewidth = 0.9) +   scale_y_continuous(breaks = 1:8) +   labs(title = "Speciation: number of distinct genetic clusters over time",        x = "Tick", y = "Number of species (clusters)") +   theme_minimal()

title: Speciation and genetic divergence
text: Running Phase 7 auto-calibration (dev/audit/calibration/) scenario’s parameter subspace discovered following regime, produces fitness improvement 189.1x defaults . See dev/audit/calibration/RESULTS.md full CMA-ES results. Expected output: n_species rises 1 2-4 genome divergence accumulates beyond isolation threshold. step staircase represents speciation event. found (2026-04-15 audit). 3 seeds × 1000 ticks, 100 agents init, 30×30 grid, isolation_threshold = 0.15. Full protocol: dev/audit/fidelity/speciation.md. Isolation threshold sweep (5 levels × 3 seeds, mut_sd = 0.2): Spearman ρ = −0.97 isolation_threshold max n_species — tighter threshold, fewer clusters, exactly theory predicts. Caveat n_species interpretation. Counts hundreds (500-agent populations) indicate pairwise-distance clustering algorithm high mutation rates flags every distinct genome separate lineage. sensitivity signs (mutation_sd isolation_threshold) correct, absolute count read “number genetic clusters detected resolution,” “Mayr-style biological species.” textbook-style 2–4 distinct species, use lower mutation (0.05–0.08) longer runs (2000+ ticks).
code: # Parameter overrides discovered by CMA-ES (see dev/audit/calibration/): s <- default_specs() s$isolation_threshold            <- 0.5127 s$mutation_sd                    <- 1.31 # env <- run_alife(s)   # uncomment to run the calibrated regime

title: Speciation and genetic divergence
text: baseline result shows n_species rising 1 2–4 genetic distance accumulates past isolation_threshold. go beyond: Speciation × disease Add disease = TRUE. Genetically distinct species share fewer contact opportunities (reproductive isolation requires spatial separation), may create inter-species barriers pathogen transmission. speciation reduce epidemic peak n_infected fragmenting contact network partially isolated sub-populations? Tried . speciation = TRUE, isolation_threshold = 0.5, transmission_prob = 0.25, 80 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42: speciation produce multiple species 200 ticks (n_species = 1 conditions); isolation_threshold = 0.5 requires genetic divergence 200 ticks allows. disease added 14% population reduction (79 vs 91 final). test fragmentation hypothesis, run ≥ 500 ticks isolation_threshold = 0.3 seed diversity via mutation_sd ≥ 0.2. Speciation × brain size Add brain_size_evolution = TRUE. incipient species diverge brain size -product ecological niche partitioning, brain size converge across species via parallel evolution toward cognitive optimum? Compare mean_brain_size across detected genetic clusters final tick. Tried . n_species = 1L, isolation_threshold = 0.3, mutation_sd = 0.1, 80 agents, 25×25 grid, 500 ticks (seed 42): n_species_max = 0 (speciation detected) brain-evolution -brain conditions, small final populations (n = 31–32). populations struggled demographically parameters — 25×25 grid 80 agents high mutation may deplete energy faster recovery. Speciation brain-size divergence compared speciation fails. experiment requires parameter tuning (larger populations, lower mutation_sd, longer runs) brain-divergence question can tested. Isolation threshold sensitivity Vary isolation_threshold 0.2 0.8 across seven values batch_alife(). speciation rate (slope n_species vs time) respond monotonically threshold, critical threshold sympatric speciation initiate even given sufficient time? Tried . Seven isolation thresholds tested (0.2 0.8; mutation_sd = 0.1, 80 agents, 300 ticks, seed 42): speciation detected threshold (n_species_max = 0 conditions). Final populations small (11–34 agents), suggesting demographic collapse primary constraint 300 ticks, isolation threshold. scenarios “found” notes speciation requires >1,000 ticks default mutation rates. Threshold sensitivity best tested ≥ 1,000 ticks minimum 100 agents mutation_sd ≥ 0.15.

title: Stress hypermutation
text: models. bacteria, severe DNA damage prolonged resource depletion triggers SOS response: suite error-prone polymerases dramatically increase genomic mutation rate (McKenzie & Rosenberg 2001). bet-hedging strategy — mutations deleterious, expanded variance offspring phenotype increases probability least one lineage escape current fitness trough. stress_hypermutation = TRUE, agents whose energy falls stress_threshold apply mutation rate multiplied stress_mutation_multiplier neural genome. Pairing scarce-resource environment (grass_rate = 0.05) ensures stress frequent enough detect effect within tractable run length. Key parameters. Expected output. Compare genetic_diversity trajectories across two conditions. stress hypermutation, diversity spike transiently resource crashes (low mean_energy epochs), followed faster adaptive recovery beneficial mutants sweep. baseline condition show slower, smoother recovery equivalent crashes.
code: library(clade) library(ggplot2)  make_s <- function(hypermut) {   s <- default_specs()   s$stress_hypermutation       <- hypermut   s$stress_threshold           <- 20.0   s$stress_mutation_multiplier <- 5.0   s$grass_rate                 <- 0.05   s$max_ticks                  <- 500L   s }  results <- batch_alife(   list(baseline = make_s(FALSE), hypermutation = make_s(TRUE)),   n_cores = 2L )  plot_df <- do.call(rbind, mapply(function(env, nm) {   d <- get_run_data(env)$ticks   data.frame(t = d$t, genetic_diversity = d$genetic_diversity, condition = nm) }, results, names(results), SIMPLIFY = FALSE))  ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = t, y = genetic_diversity, colour = condition)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.7, alpha = 0.8) +   scale_colour_manual(values = c(baseline = "#878787",                                  hypermutation = "#d6604d")) +   labs(     title    = "Stress hypermutation increases genetic diversity during resource crashes",     subtitle = "grass_rate = 0.05; stress_mutation_multiplier = 5",     x = "Tick", y = "Genetic diversity", colour = "Condition"   ) +   theme_minimal()

title: Stress hypermutation
text: Running Phase 7 auto-calibration (dev/audit/calibration/) scenario’s parameter subspace discovered following regime, produces fitness improvement 3.8x defaults . See dev/audit/calibration/RESULTS.md full CMA-ES results. Expected output: genetic diversity transiently elevated stress-hypermutation condition resource-crash epochs. Adaptive recovery faster hypermutation condition beneficial variants spread rapidly population. found. Running 3 replicates stress_hypermutation = TRUE vs FALSE, 80 agents, 25×25 grid, grass_rate = 0.15, 400 ticks (seeds 41–43): conditions produced essentially identical results — mean population 207 vs 206, genetic diversity 0.2500 vs 0.2497, mean energy 125.4 vs 125.7. absence detectable effect expected: stress_energy_threshold = 40.0 grass_rate = 0.15, agents maintain energy well 40 throughout run, stress condition (low energy) rarely triggers. Stress hypermutation produces visible effects resource scarcity (grass_rate ≤ 0.05), substantial fraction population dips threshold, resource crash. stress_mutation_sd_multiplier = 5.0 parameter ensures stress active, mutations order magnitude larger baseline — intended regime observing SOS-response analogue.
code: # Parameter overrides discovered by CMA-ES (see dev/audit/calibration/): s <- default_specs() s$stress_threshold               <- 5L s$stress_mutation_multiplier     <- 0L s$grass_rate                     <- 3e-04 # env <- run_alife(s)   # uncomment to run the calibrated regime

title: Stress hypermutation
text: baseline result shows stress hypermutation transiently elevates genetic diversity resource crashes accelerates adaptive recovery. go beyond: Kin buffering hypermutation cost Add kin_selection = TRUE. Kin altruism provides energy buffer may reduce often agents fall stress_threshold, dampening hypermutation response. kin selection reduce frequency hypermutation events variance genetic_diversity spikes? Plot spike height vs kin_altruism_r_min across batch_alife(). Tried . stress_hypermutation = TRUE, grass_rate = 0.05, 60 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42: genetic diversity SD = 0.0116 without kin vs 0.0175 kin — kin selection increased diversity spike height. Mean diversity also rose 0.181 0.199. Counter prediction: kin energy transfers dampen hypermutation. Spatially clustered kin groups may concentrate stress events locally, producing sharper (smoother) diversity bursts. Hypermutation × seasonality Add seasonal_amplitude = 0.7. Resource crashes winter trigger hypermutation; timing diversity spikes align seasonal grass trough? Plot genetic_diversity grass_coverage shared axes test synchrony. Tried . stress_hypermutation = TRUE seasonal_amplitude = 0.7 (50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): stress hypermutation condition showed gd = 0.192 vs 0.185 without. mean_mutation_rate field returned NA — Julia backend log hypermutation event timing — direct synchrony testing unavailable current logging. diversity increase (3.8%) consistent seasonal troughs triggering stress hypermutation, tick-level alignment confirmed without additional logging support. Evolved mutation rate Enable mutation_rate_evolution = TRUE. population evolve lower baseline mutation_sd hypermutation available stress-contingent backup? hypermutation analogue Baldwin Effect — contingent machinery substituting constitutive mutation cost. Tried . flags enabled (50 agents, 200 ticks, seed 42): gd = 0.188 vs 0.185 without mutation rate evolution. mean_mutation_rate column returned NA — logged Julia backend — canalization prediction (evolved lower baseline mutation contingent hypermutation available) confirmed. diversity difference consistent additional mutational input, Baldwin-like substitution mechanism untestable current metrics.

what: clade: Agent-Based Evolutionary Simulation with Julia Backend — clade-package
title: clade: Agent-Based Evolutionary Simulation with Julia Backend — clade-package
text: clade provides agent-based simulations evolution, foraging ecology, social behaviour. R layer interface; per-tick simulation kernel runs Julia via JuliaConnectoR, R↔Julia boundary crossed exactly per run_alife() call.

title: clade: Agent-Based Evolutionary Simulation with Julia Backend — clade-package
text: First call run_alife() precompiles Julia kernel (~10–90 s, per Julia session).
code: specs <- default_specs() specs$n_agents_init <- 40L specs$max_ticks     <- 300L env  <- run_alife(specs) data <- get_run_data(env) plot_run(data)

title: clade: Agent-Based Evolutionary Simulation with Julia Backend — clade-package
text: default_specs() — canonical parameter list; modify pass run_alife(). run_alife() / run_clade() — run single simulation. batch_alife() — run many specs lists (parallel via parallel::mclapply). get_run_data() — extract $ticks $deaths data frames env. plot_run() — population / energy / diversity dashboard. search_cmaes() / search_map_elites() / search_gradient() — parameter search driven user-supplied fitness function. register_module() — attach custom per-tick R hook (rarely needed — modules live Julia side).

title: clade: Agent-Based Evolutionary Simulation with Julia Backend — clade-package
text: modules disabled default enabled single flag specs list. See README.md module table vignettes/parameter-reference.Rmd full list defaults expected effects.

title: clade: Agent-Based Evolutionary Simulation with Julia Backend — clade-package
text: Set specs$brain_type: "bnn" (default) — Bayesian neural network Thompson-sampled weights REINFORCE posterior updates (Williams 1992; Blundell et al. 2015). "ann" — standard multilayer perceptron. "ctrnn" — continuous-time recurrent network (Beer 1995). "grn" — sparse gene-regulatory network topology. "transformer" — self-attention (highest capacity, slowest). "synthesis" — symbolic rule extraction evolved weights.

what: Compute pedigree-based relatedness between two agents — compute_relatedness
title: Compute pedigree-based relatedness between two agents — compute_relatedness
text: compute_relatedness() estimates coefficient relatedness (r) two agents using parent-ID pedigree chains stored env$agents. algorithm returns r = 0.5 parent-offspring, r = 0.25 full siblings (shared parent), r = 0 otherwise. matches Hamilton's rule coefficients used kin selection module.

title: Default simulation parameters for clade — default_specs
